Additionally, they must possess the relevant expertise and professional skills necessary to provide informed and objective input on the specific content areas covered by the Stability Scope.

core_children.core_name: BA Labs Conflict Of Interest Waiver
core_children.core_content:
BA Labs is granted a limited waiver to the rule prohibiting Stability Scope Advisors from engaging in other business activities that could result in a conflict of interest. This waiver applies only to BA Labs’ work with Ethena. This waiver provides Sky with greater transparency and responsiveness in the event of any issue with Ethena. It also allows BA Labs to align Ethena’s risk strategy with Sky.

However, should additional Stability Scope Advisors be appointed, they will be required to assume full responsibility for all risk management activities associated with Ethena.

section_primary_docs.core_content:
The currently active Scope Advisors are:
 
BA Labs

ETH Address: 0xDfe08A40054685E205Ed527014899d1EDe49B892

scope_articles.article_content: This Article defines methodologies and processes for optimizing and aligning the Core Stability Parameters, which are designed to stabilize the USDS Stablecoin.

article_sections.section_content: This Section defines the role of the Stability Facilitators in managing the Core Stability Parameters.

section_primary_docs.core_content: Prior to the launch of Agent Vaults and the implementation of a new Rate System, all Stability Parameters related to Sky Core and Spark will be managed by the Stability Facilitators, in consultation with the Stability Scope Advisors. This process will be conducted through the Operational Weekly Governance Cycle or, if necessary, through out-of-schedule Executive Votes.

As long as Spark Protocol remains near its maximum borrow limit, the Stability Facilitators can use the Operational Weekly Governance Cycle to propose to increase its Debt Ceiling to attract more users.

article_sections.section_content: This Section defines the role of the Core Executor Agents in managing the Core Stability Parameters.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The Core Executor Agents, in consultation with the Stability Facilitators and the Stability Scope Advisors, must set the Base Rate to ensure (1) a sufficient level of Cash Stablecoins and (2) price stability of USDS and other Stablecoins produced by the Sky Ecosystem. The subdocuments herein define the process that the Core Executor Agents should follow in setting the Base Rate.

core_children.core_content: The Core Executor Agents should modify the Base Rate on a daily basis, as necessary, in consultation with the Stability Facilitators and Stability Scope Advisors. In doing so, the Core Executor Agents should consider the factors specified in the documents herein.

The Core Executor Agents should consider the external interest-rate environment, including the interest rates offered by competitors and funding rates in decentralized finance and traditional finance markets.
The Core Executor Agents must develop automated tools to monitor the external interest-rate environment to improve the rate-setting process.

The Core Executor Agents should consider other factors such as supporting long-term market stability, user stability, system sustainability, and growth. In addition, the Core Executor Agents should consider constraints on Stars, including their cost of capital and reaction time, to avoid creating unnecessary volatility in rates.

section_primary_docs.core_content: When setting the Base Rate, the Core Executor Agents must also set all other Core Stability Parameters so that they maintain their specified relationship to the Base Rate. See A.3.2.2 - Parameters. The only exception is the Dai Savings Rate, which must be gradually reduced to 0% as specified in A.3.2.2.4.1 - Dai Savings Rate Modification.

The smart contracts that implement all of the Core Stability Parameters must be configured to allow the Core Executor Agents to modify the Core Stability Parameters using the Stability Parameter Bounded External Access Module. See A.3.8.1.2 - Stability Parameter Bounded External Access Module.

article_sections.section_content: This Section defines the Core Stability Parameters.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The Base Rate is the key interest rate in the system. It defines all other rates by various spreads. It is expressed as an annual percentage rate.

core_children.core_name: Long Term Base Rate Determination Process
core_children.core_content: In the long term, the Base Rate must be automatically determined based on a model which ensures long term price stability of USDS and other Stablecoins produced by the Sky Ecosystem.

core_children.core_name: Medium Term Base Rate Determination Process
core_children.core_content:
In the medium term, the Base Rate is changed by Executive Votes proposed by the Stability Facilitators based on the recommendation of the Stability Scope Advisors.

The Stability Facilitators must regularly update the Base Rate in order to ensure (1) a sufficient level of Cash Stablecoins and (2) price stability of USDS and other Stablecoins produced by the Ecosystem.
Spreads which are attached to the Base Rate can also be changed through the Operational Weekly Governance Cycle.

core_annotations.annotation_content: The element refers to the differences added or subtracted from the Base Rate to determine other specific rates.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The Dai Savings Rate (”DSR”) is the rate Dai holders can earn on their Dai in the Dai Savings Rate smart contracts.

core_children.core_content:
The Dai Savings Rate can be modified through either Executive Votes or the Stability Parameter Bounded External Access Module. See A.3.8.1.2 - Stability Parameter Bounded External Access Module. 

The Dai Savings Rate must gradually be reduced to 0% over time.

core_children.core_name: Dai Savings Rate Stability Parameter Bounded External Access Module Parameters
core_children.core_content:
The Stability Parameter Bounded External Access Module parameters for the Dai Savings Rate are:
• [code]max[/code] - 3,000 basis points,
• [code]min[/code] - 0 basis points,
• [code]step[/code] - 400 basis points,
• [code]tau[/code] - Globally defined in A.3.8.1.2.1.4.1 - Tau Current Value.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The Sky Savings Rate (”SSR”) is the rate USDS holders can earn on their USDS in the Sky Savings Rate smart contracts.

core_children.core_content: The Sky Savings Rate can be modified through either Executive Votes or the Stability Parameter Bounded External Access Module. See A.3.8.1.2 - Stability Parameter Bounded External Access Module.

core_children.core_name: Sky Savings Rate Stability Parameter Bounded External Access Module Parameters
core_children.core_content:
The Stability Parameter Bounded External Access Module parameters for the Sky Savings Rate are:
• [code]max[/code] - 3,000 basis points,
• [code]min[/code] - 200 basis points,
• [code]step[/code] - 400 basis points,
• [code]tau[/code] - Globally defined in A.3.8.1.2.1.4.1 - Tau Current Value.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The Agent Rate is the rate that Star Agents earn on unrewarded USDS, Dai, and sUSDS balances that they hold.

core_children.core_content: The Agent Rate is 0.05% below the Base Rate. This is equal to the Sky Savings Rate plus (1) the 0.1% Accessibility Reward Fee (see A.2.3.8.1.2.1.3.1 - Fee For USDS and sUSDS Balances) and (2) the 0.1% Star Agent Management Fee (see A.2.3.8.1.2.1.3.2 - Star Agent Management Fee).

core_children.core_name: Treatment Of USDS and Dai Balances
core_children.core_content: Star Agents receive the full Agent Rate on unrewarded USDS and Dai balances through the Monthly Settlement Cycle, as specified in A.3.2.2.3.6 - Settlement.

Star Agents receive the Agent Rate on sUSDS balances in a modified form. sUSDS balances already earn the Sky Savings Rate. In addition to this, sUSDS balances earn additional compensation of 0.20% to account for the 0.1% Accessibility Reward Fee (see A.2.3.8.1.2.1.3.1 - Fee For USDS and sUSDS Balances) and the 0.1% Star Agent Management Fee (see A.2.3.8.1.2.1.3.2 - Star Agent Management Fee) that is paid through the Monthly Settlement Cycle, as specified in A.3.2.2.3.6 - Settlement.

Spark is entitled to a higher Agent Rate equal to the Base Rate on USDS and sUSDS balances held in Peg Stability Modules. See A.3.4.2.1.1 - Peg Stability Module. The Agent Rate earned by Spark is still subject to the limitations set forth in A.3.2.2.3.5 - Limitations For Star Agents Receiving Subsidized Rate.

core_children.core_name: Limitations For Star Agents Receiving Subsidized Rate
core_children.core_content: If a Star Agent is borrowing funds from Sky at a Subsidized Rate, as defined in A.3.2.2.5.2 - Subsidized Rate, then the Agent Rate earned by that Star Agent shall be limited. Specifically, if the standard Agent Rate would exceed the Star Agent’s Subsidized Rate, the Star Agent will earn the Subsidized Rate on those balances. The difference between what would have been the standard Agent Rate earnings and the earnings at the Subsidized Rate is effectively retained by Sky. This adjustment is processed as part of the Monthly Settlement Cycle, as specified in A.3.2.2.3.6 - Settlement. If the standard Agent Rate is less than or equal to the Subsidized Rate, the standard Agent Rate applies.

Any compensation due to or from Star Agents so that they earn the Agent Rate on USDS, Dai, and sUSDS balances is paid as part of the Monthly Settlement Cycle. See A.2.5 - Sky Core Monthly Settlement Cycle.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The Agent Credit Line Borrow Rate is the annual percentage rate that Agents must pay to Sky Core to receive USDS liquidity into their respective vaults.

core_children.core_name: Agents Credit Line Borrow Rate Risk Limits
core_children.core_content: USDS liquidity that Agents receive from Sky Core can be deployed across Conduits within risk limits defined by the Stability Facilitators.

scope_articles.article_content: Star Agents who invest capital from Sky’s Collateral Portfolio using the Allocation System Primitive must hold Risk Capital to protect Sky from potential losses on these investments. This Article sets forth the framework governing Risk Capital.

article_sections.section_content: This Section defines the conceptual framework for Risk Capital.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define the conceptual framework for determining the amount of Risk Capital that Star Agents are required to hold to protect Sky from risks on their investments.

core_children.core_content: The documents herein define how Risk Capital required for different categories of risk are calculated and combined to determine total Risk Capital required.

Different risk factors may apply to varying degrees to particular types of investments. For example, investments through decentralized lending markets are subject to credit risk but not legal risk. As such, the risk framework contains methodologies for calculating the Risk Capital required with respect to different risk factors. The Risk Capital requirements for each risk factor relevant to an investment are then added together to arrive at the total Risk Capital required.
The documents herein provide an overview of the process for estimating Risk Capital for different types of investments.

For each type of investment, the relevant risk factors should be determined. This determination should be based on a fundamental analysis of the extent to which each of these risk factors is relevant to the investment.
For each risk factor that is relevant to an investment, the required Risk Capital to protect Sky from potential losses on that investment should be estimated.
Once the Risk Capital requirements associated with each risk factor are calculated, they should be added to arrive at a total Risk Capital requirement.
The near term focus of the framework is on lending markets such as SparkLend. For lending markets, the relevant risk factors are financial risk, smart contract risk, and admin risk.
Additional risk factors, including legal risk and oracle risk, will be incorporated in future iterations of the risk framework.

The documents herein define the conceptual framework for determining the Risk Capital required to protect Sky from financial risk arising from investments by Star Agents of funds from Sky’s Collateral Portfolio. The financial risk framework is currently focused on credit risk. In future iterations of the risk framework, financial risk will be disaggregated into credit risk, liquidity risk, and market risk.
Credit risk is the risk that a loan or other debt instrument will not be repaid according to agreed terms.

In lending markets, an asset originally posted as collateral may be subsequently lent as collateral for additional loans, increasing overall exposure. Methodologies for estimating risk should take into account this overall exposure. To the extent that other investments have similar mechanisms that can increase overall risk, these should be incorporated into the calculation of required Risk Capital.

To capture exposures related to rehypothecation, the Risk Capital framework utilizes the concept of “depth levels”. The first depth level relates to exposures that have not been rehypothecated. The second depth level relates to additional exposures from the rehypothecated portion of first depth level exposures. The possibility of further lending from re-lent positions should be accounted for, either as an adjustment to the second depth level exposure or through incorporation of additional depth levels.
Effective exposures should reflect both non-rehypothecated exposures from the first depth level as well as rehypothecated exposures from the second depth level.
The documents herein define core metrics that will be used in the Financial Risk framework.
The Probability Of Default is the probability that a loan will default. This is an important variable in determining Risk Capital required for Credit Risk.

The Probability Of Default is currently estimated assuming that returns of the underlying asset are normally distributed. Future iterations of the risks framework should explore other probability distributions to better incorporate “fat tails” in asset returns.

The Loss Given Default is the estimate of the loss that will occur on a loan conditional on it having defaulted. It is typically estimated at some confidence level, for example that in 99.9% of cases, the actual loss will not exceed the specified value. This is an important variable in determining Risk Capital Required for Credit Risk.

The Loss Given Default is currently estimated assuming that returns of the underlying asset are normally distributed. Future iterations of the risk framework should explore other probability distributions to better incorporate “fat tails” in asset returns.
Asset Correlation is a factor that accounts for the fact that defaults on loans are not completely correlated, so some loans in a portfolio may default while others continue to perform.

The current implementation of Asset Correlation uses parameters from the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision that were estimated for commercial loans (https://www.bis.org/bcbs/irbriskweight.pdf). These should be replaced by parameters that are based on historical correlation of crypto loans in a future iteration of the risk framework.

The Risk Capital required for Credit Risk is a function of the Probability of Default, the Loss Given Default, and the Asset Correlation. The methodology for calculating Risk Capital required for Credit Risk based on these variables is specified in the implementation of the Risk Capital framework.

The documents herein define the conceptual framework for determining the Risk Capital required to protect Sky from smart contract risk arising from investments by Star Agents of funds from Sky’s Collateral Portfolio.

Smart Contract Risk refers to the risk that an investment through the Allocation System will experience a loss of funds due to a bug or technical exploit. This risk may arise from the protocol invested in or other aspects of the investment such as bridging requirements.

The conceptual framework for smart contract risk is still being developed, so Star Agents are not currently required to hold any Risk Capital for Smart Contract Risk; they are only required to hold Risk Capital for Credit Risk.

core_children.core_name: Short Term Review By Operational GovOps
core_children.core_content: Until the framework for Smart Contract Risk has been implemented, Operational Executor Agents can decline to operationalize any Allocation System Primitive deployment that they deem poses excessive smart contract risk, unless the Agent maintains 100% Risk Capital for that deployment.

The documents herein define the conceptual framework for determining the Risk Capital required to protect Sky from admin risk arising from investments by Star Agents of funds from Sky’s Collateral Portfolio.

Admin Risk refers to the risk that an investment through the Allocation System will experience a loss of funds due to abuse of privileged access to a protocol. This risk may arise from a multisig or another actor that has the ability to modify the protocol or access funds without going through a governance process.

The conceptual framework for admin risk is still being developed, so Star Agents are not currently required to hold any Risk Capital for Admin Risk; they are only required to hold Risk Capital for Credit Risk.

core_children.core_name: Short Term Review By Operational GovOps
core_children.core_content: Until the framework for Admin Risk has been implemented, Operational Executor Agents can decline to operationalize any Allocation System Primitive deployment that they deem poses excessive admin risk, unless the Agent maintains 100% Risk Capital for that deployment.
In the near term, the Risk Capital framework applies only to capital deployments into lending markets such as SparkLend. The framework will be expanded to cover additional asset classes over time.
The documents herein specify the near term logic regarding Risk Capital requirements for types of assets other than lending markets.
The documents herein define types of exposures that are either restricted or completely prohibited until the conceptual framework and its implementation are expanded to include them.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define the conceptual framework for eligible assets that Star Agents may hold that count toward their required Risk Capital. Agents failing to maintain the required level of capital will be subject to penalties.

core_children.core_content: Risk Capital comprises two main categories based on loss absorption seniority: First Loss Capital (FLC) and Senior Risk Capital (SRC), further divided by source.

The documents herein define the required relationship and constraints between the different types of Risk Capital. These ensure that the Star Agent maintains the appropriate level of Internal First Loss Capital relative to its External and Senior capital exposures, ensuring sufficient “skin in the game” with respect to its investments.

article_sections.section_content: This Section defines the current implementation of the Risk Capital framework.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define the implementation of the risk framework for calculating required Risk Capital.
The documents herein define the implementation of the financial risk framework.

core_children.core_content: The documents herein define implementations of the financial risk framework for specific asset classes. In the near term, the only implementation that is defined is for lending markets. In the future additional implementations will be added herein.
The documents herein define the implementation of the financial risk framework for lending markets.

The documents herein define the calculation of the Risk Capital required with respect to a Star Agent’s investments. Unless otherwise specified, code is in Python. Alternative implementations may be used but these implementations must produce the same outputs as the reference implementation included herein.
The documents herein define the variables that are used in the calculation of required Risk Capital.

All floating point variables are specified to four decimal places of precision. For example, 100 million would be represented as [code]100000000.0000[/code] and 75% would be represented as [code]0.7500[/code].

Input variables are variables that are input into the model for calculating required Risk Capital, either based on on-chain or off-chain data or reflecting assumptions or parameters set by Governance.

The [code]underlying[/code] variable is a string identifying the asset borrowed in a particular lending market. For example, if Dai is borrowed in a lending market, then the value of the [code]underlying[/code] for that market would be [code]DAI[/code].

The [code]collateral_name[/code] variable is a string identifying a particular collateral asset. For example, if WETH is used as collateral, then the value of the [code]collateral_name[/code] for that collateral would be [code]WETH[/code].

The [code]debt_backed[/code] variable is a floating point number indicating the amount of the underlying asset borrowed in a market backed by a particular collateral. It is calculated by multiplying (1) the dollar amount of the underlying asset borrowed by (2) the ratio of the dollar value of the collateral to the dollar value of all collaterals across all markets. For example, if there are $30 million of Dai borrowings and $70 million of USDC borrowings that are collectively backed by $240 million of WETH and $60 million of WBTC, then [code]debt_backed[/code] for the Dai market and the WETH collateral would be $24 million.

The [code]collateral[/code] variable is a floating point number indicating the amount of a particular collateral for a market. It is calculated by multiplying (1) the dollar amount of the collateral by (2) the ratio of dollar borrowings in the market to the dollar value of borrowings across all markets. For example, if there are $30 million of Dai borrowings and $70 million of USDC borrowings that are collectively backed by $240 million of WETH and $60 million of WBTC, then [code]collateral[/code] for the Dai market and the WETH collateral would be $72 million.
The [code]liquidation_threshold[/code] is a floating point number indicating the loan to value point at which liquidation is triggered in a lending market.
The [code]liquidation_penalty[/code] is a floating point number representing the percentage of the amount liquidated that is paid to the actor that liquidates an unhealthy loan in a lending market.

The [code]liquidation_efficiency_factor[/code] is an estimate of what percent of the fair market value of the collateral can be recovered in liquidation. The [code]liquidation_efficiency_factor[/code] is currently set to 75% for all collaterals.
The [code]volatility[/code] for a collateral is a floating point number defined as the standard deviation of daily price changes for the collateral over the last 30 days.

The [code]T[/code] variable is a floating point number indicating the time horizon, in years, over which the Probability Of Default is being estimated. [code]T[/code] is currently set to one (1) year.
Derived variables are variables that are calculated based on other variables.

The [code]liquidation_ratio[/code] is the value of the collateral for a loan as a percentage of the amount borrowed below which liquidation will be triggered. It is the inverse of the [code]liquidation_threshold[/code].

The [code]rehypothecation_factor[/code] is the ratio of total borrowing and total collateral for an asset. Total borrowing for an asset is defined as the sum of [code]debt_backed[/code] where that asset is the [code]underlying[/code] for all values of [code]collateral_name[/code]. The total collateral is defined as the sum of the [code]collateral[/code] variable where the [code]collateral_name[/code] corresponds to the asset across all values of [code]underlying[/code].

The [code]relative_debt_backed[/code] for an [code]underlying[/code] and a [code]collateral_name[/code] is the [code]debt_backed[/code] for that [code]underlying[/code] and [code]collateral_name[/code] divided by the sum of the [code]debt_backed[/code] for all [code]collateral_name[/code] values for the [code]underlying[/code]. For example, if there are $100 million of Dai borrowings, of which $80 million are backed by WETH and $20 million are backed by WBTC, the [code]relative_debt_backed[/code] for the Dai market and the WETH collateral would be 80%.

The [code]relative_collateral[/code] is the [code]collateral[/code] for a particular [code]underlying[/code] and [code]collateral_name[/code] divided by the sum of the values for all [code]collateral[/code] for that [code]underlying[/code]. For example, if $100 million of Dai borrowings are backed by $300 million of WETH and $100 million of WBTC, the value of the [code]relative_collateral[/code] for WETH would be 75%.
The first stage of the calculation process is calculating effective exposures for each asset, including both first order exposures and second order exposures from rehypothecation.

The process begins with a single table where each row corresponds to a single combination of asset being borrowed ([code]underlying[/code]) and backing collateral ([code]collateral_name[/code]). Each row has the following fields:
    
◦ [code]underlying[/code],
◦ [code]collateral_name[/code],
◦ [code]debt_backed[/code],
◦ [code]rehypothecation_factor[/code],
◦ [code]collateral[/code],
◦ [code]relative_debt_backed[/code],
◦ [code]relative_collateral[/code]
The documents herein define the First Level Computation of exposures, which breaks down exposures into non-rehypothecated and rehypothecated exposures.

The calculations specified in the documents below should be performed for each combination of asset being borrowed ([code]underlying[/code]) and backing collateral ([code]collateral_name[/code]). The current value of the [code]collateral_name[/code] for each calculation is referred to as [code]collateral_1[/code].
The Level 1 Rehypothecation Factor ([code]reh_factor_1[/code]) is set equal to the [code]rehypothecation_factor[/code].
The Level 1 Collateralization ([code]coll_1[/code]) is set equal to the [code]collateralization[/code].

The Level 1 Base Exposure ([code]base_exposure_1[/code]) is calculated as the [code]debt_backed[/code] times one minus [code]reh_factor_1[/code]. This represents the portion of the exposure that is not rehypothecated.

The Level 1 Rehypothecated Exposure ([code]reh_exposure_1[/code]) is calculated as the [code]debt_backed[/code] times [code]reh_factor_1[/code]. This represents the portion of the exposure that is rehypothecated.

The Effective Exposure for the collateral ([code]effective_exposures[collateral_1][/code]) is set equal to [code]base_exposure_1[/code]. This will later be increased as part of the Second Level Computation.

The Debt Backed Exposure for the collateral ([code]debt_backed_exposures[collateral_1][/code]) is set equal to [code]debt_backed[/code]. This will later be increased as part of the Second Level Computation.
The Collateralization for the collateral ([code]collateralization[collateral_1][/code]) is set equal to [code]collateral[/code]. This will later be increased as part of the Second Level Computation.
The documents herein define the Second Level Computation of exposures, which calculates additional exposures due to rehypothecation.

The calculations specified in the documents below should be performed for each asset being borrowed [code]underlying[/code] and backing collateral [code]collateral_name[/code]. The current value of the [code]collateral_name[/code] for each calculation is referred to as [code]collateral_1[/code]. For each combination of [code]underlying[/code] and [code]collateral_name[/code], the calculation should be performed for each row where the [code]underlying[/code] is equal to [code]collateral_1[/code]. The [code]collateral_name[/code] in such row is referred to as [code]collateral_2[/code]. This considers the hypothecation exposures where an asset that is collateral is also being borrowed.
The Level 2 Relative Backing ([code]relative_backing_2[/code]) is set to the [code]relative_backing[/code] for [code]underlying[/code] and [code]collateral_2[/code].
The Level 2 Collateralization Factor ([code]coll_factor_2[/code]) is set to the [code]relative_collateral[/code] for [code]underlying[/code] and [code]collateral_2[/code].

The Level 2 Effective Exposure ([code]effective_exposure_2[/code]) is calculated as the product of the Level 1 Rehypothecated Exposure ([code]reh_exposure_1[/code]) and the Level 2 Relative Backing, ([code]relative_backing_2[/code]), scaled up by one plus the Level 1 Rehypothecation Factor ([code]reh_factor_1[/code]).

The Level 2 Debt Backed ([code]debt_backed_2[/code]) is calculated as the product of the Level 1 Rehypothecated Exposure ([code]reh_exposure_1[/code]) and the Level 2 Relative Backing ([code]relative_backing_2[/code]).

The Level 2 Collateralization ([code]coll_backed_2[/code]) is calculated as the product of the Level 1 Collateral, [code]coll_1[/code] and the Level 2 Collateralization Factor, [code]coll_factor_2[/code].
The Effective Exposure for [code]collateral_2[/code] ([code]effective_exposures[collateral_2][/code]) is incremented by [code]effective_exposure_2[/code].
The Debt Backed Exposure for [code]collateral_2 [/code]([code]debt_backed_exposures[collateral_2][/code]) is incremented by [code]debt_backed_2[/code].
The Collateralization for the [code]collateral_2 [/code]([code]collateralization[collateral_2][/code]) is incremented by [code]coll_backed_2[/code].
The documents herein define the process for aggregating the results from the First Level Computation and Second Level Computation and structuring them for use in later calculations.

core_children.core_name: Collect Outputs From First Level Computation And Second Level Computation
core_children.core_content: The final values of [code]effective_exposures[/code], [code]debt_backed_exposures[/code], and [code]collateralization[/code] are collected into a new table with one row for each combination of [code]underlying[/code] and [code]collateral_name[/code]. The fields corresponding to [code]effective_exposures[/code], [code]debt_backed_exposures[/code], and [code]collateralization[/code] are renamed [code]effective_exposure[/code], [code]debt_backed[/code], and [code]effective_coll[/code], respectively. [code]underlying[/code] and [code]collateral_name[/code] are renamed [code]underlying_symbol[/code] and [code]final_collateral[/code], respectively.
The table created above is enriched with the [code]liquidation_ratio[/code], [code]liquidation_penalty[/code], [code]rehypothecation_factor[/code], and [code]volatility[/code] variables.

A new renormalized [code]relative_backing[/code] field is calculated by dividing the [code]effective_exposure[/code] value for each row by the total [code]effective_exposure[/code] value for all rows with the same [code]underlying[/code].

• As a result of the calculations the final output of this step is a table with one row for each combination of asset being borrowed ([code]underlying_symbol[/code]) and backing collateral ([code]final_collateral[/code]). Each row has the following fields:
    
◦ [code]underlying_symbol[/code],
◦ [code]final_collateral[/code],
◦ [code]effective_exposure[/code],
◦ [code]debt_backed[/code],
◦ [code]effective_coll[/code],
◦ [code]liquidation_ratio[/code],
◦ [code]liquidation_penalty[/code],
◦ [code]rehypothecation_factor[/code],
◦ [code]relative_backing[/code],
◦ [code]volatility[/code]

An implementation of the calculation of effective exposures in Python is shown below:

[code]for underlying in all_underlyings:
    level1 = df[df['underlying_symbol'] == underlying].copy()

    # Dictionaries to accumulate exposures by collateral.
    effective_exposures = {}
    debt_backed_exposures = {}
    collateralization = {}
    
   for _, row1 in level1.iterrows():
        collateral_1  = row1['collateral_name']
        debt_1        = row1['debt_backed']
        reh_factor_1  = row1['rehypothecation_factor']
        coll_1        = row1['collateral']

        # (A) Non-rehypothecated
        base_exposure_1 = debt_1 * (1.0 - reh_factor_1)
        effective_exposures[collateral_1] = effective_exposures.get(collateral_1, 0.0) + base_exposure_1
        debt_backed_exposures[collateral_1] = debt_backed_exposures.get(collateral_1, 0.0) + debt_1
        collateralization[collateral_1] = collateralization.get(collateral_1, 0.0) + coll_1

        # (B) Rehypothecated
        reh_exposure_1 = debt_1 * reh_factor_1
        # fraction of the "coll_1" that is also being re-lent
        collateralization_1 = coll_1 * reh_factor_1
        
        level2 = df[df['underlying_symbol'] == collateral_1]
        for _, row2 in level2.iterrows():
            collateral_2 = row2['collateral_name']
            relative_backing2 = row2['relative_debt_backed']
            coll_factor2 = row2['relative_collateral']

            # Depth=2 effective exposure
            effective_exposure_2 = reh_exposure_1 * relative_backing2 * (1.0 + reh_factor_1)
            effective_exposures[collateral_2] = (
                effective_exposures.get(collateral_2, 0.0) + effective_exposure_2
            )

            # Depth=2 debt backed
            debt_backed_2 = reh_exposure_1 * relative_backing2
            debt_backed_exposures[collateral_2] = (
                debt_backed_exposures.get(collateral_2, 0.0) + debt_backed_2
            )

            # Depth=2 collateralization
            coll_backed_1 = collateralization_1 * coll_factor2
            collateralization[collateral_2] = (
                collateralization.get(collateral_2, 0.0) + coll_backed_1
            )
            
   # Combine keys from our three dictionaries:
    all_collaterals = set(effective_exposures.keys()) | set(debt_backed_exposures.keys())

    for coll_name in all_collaterals:
        res = {
            'underlying_symbol': underlying,
            'final_collateral': coll_name,
            'effective_exposure': effective_exposures.get(coll_name, 0.0),
            'debt_backed': debt_backed_exposures.get(coll_name, 0.0),
            'effective_coll': collateralization.get(coll_name, 0.0)
        }

        results.append(res)[/code]
The second stage of the calculation process involves calculating other effective parameters taking into account the impact of rehypothecation.

The documents below define the process for calculating other effective parameters for a single row of the output table from the previous step, corresponding to a combination of an underlying market ([code]underlying_symbol[/code]) and collateral ([code]final_collateral[/code]).
The documents herein define the process for computing the Effective Volatility.

The Effective Volatility ([code]effective_volatility[/code]) is calculated for each combination of underlying market and backing collateral using the Python function specified below, where [code]row[/code] is one row from the output table from calculating effective exposures and [code]df[/code] is the entire table. This combines the volatility for the non-rehypothecated portion of the collateral with the volatility from the rehypothecated portion of the collateral, based on the volatility of the collaterals that back the rehypothecated portion.

[code]def calc_effective_volatility(row, df):
    """
    Compute effective volatility.
    """
    w_top = row['relative_backing']           # The fraction of the total backing contributed by this collateral at the top level
    f_top = row['rehypothecation_factor']     # How much of this collateral is rehypothecated
    top_vol = row['volatility']               # The top-level collateral's own volatility
    
    # Non-rehypothecated portion
    non_reh_vol = (1 - f_top) * top_vol
    
    # 'M_top' is the magnitude of the portion that is rehypothecated
    M_top = w_top * f_top

    # Find "child" rows where this row's final_collateral is itself an underlying_symbol
    backing = df[df['underlying_symbol'] == row['final_collateral']].copy()
    if backing.empty:
        # If there are no deeper levels for this collateral, the effective volatility is just the top-level's volatility
        return top_vol

    # For each child, define an effective weight (w_eff) = (M_top * [child's relative_backing * child's rehypothecation_factor])
    backing['w_eff'] = M_top * (backing['relative_backing'] * backing['rehypothecation_factor'])
    
    # We model the 'combined' volatility by summing variances from each child, i.e. w_eff^2 * volatility^2
    backing['var_contrib'] = (backing['w_eff'] ** 2) * (backing['volatility'] ** 2)
    sigma_backing = np.sqrt(backing['var_contrib'].sum())
    
    # Final effective volatility = sum of top's non_rehypothecated portion + child-level sqrt of variance
    return sigma_backing + non_reh_vol
[/code]
The documents herein define the process for computing the Effective Liquidation Ratio.

The Effective Liquidation Ratio ([code]effective_liquidation_ratio[/code]) is calculated for each combination of underlying market and backing collateral using the Python function specified below, where [code]row[/code] is one row from the output table from calculating effective exposures and [code]df[/code] is the entire table. This combines the liquidation ratio for the non-rehypothecated portion of the collateral with the liquidation ratio for the rehypothecated portion of the collateral, based on the liquidation ratio of the collaterals that back the rehypothecated portion.

[code]def calc_effective_liquidation_ratio(row, df):
    """
    Compute the effective liquidation ratio.
    """
    w_top = row['relative_backing']
    f_top = row['rehypothecation_factor']
    top_liq = row['liquidation_ratio']
    
    # Non-rehypothecated portion (straight percentage of top-level ratio)
    non_reh_liq = (1 - f_top) * top_liq
    M_top = w_top * f_top
    
    # Locate child rows
    backing = df[df['underlying_symbol'] == row['final_collateral']].copy()
    if backing.empty:
        return top_liq

    # Weighted portion for child rows
    backing['w_eff'] = M_top * (backing['relative_backing'] * backing['rehypothecation_factor'])
    liq_contrib = (backing['w_eff'] * backing['liquidation_ratio']).sum()
    
    return non_reh_liq + liq_contrib[/code]
The documents herein define the process for computing the Effective Liquidation Penalty.

The Effective Liquidation Penalty ([code]effective_liquidation_penalty[/code]) is calculated for each combination of underlying market and backing collateral using the Python function specified below, where [code]row[/code] is one row from the output table from calculating effective exposures and [code]df[/code] is the entire table. This combines the liquidation penalty for the non-rehypothecated portion of the collateral with the liquidation penalty for the rehypothecated portion of the collateral, based on the liquidation penalty for the collaterals that back the rehypothecated portion.

[code]def calc_effective_liquidation_penalty(row, df):
    """
    Compute the effective liquidation penalty.
    """
    w_top = row['relative_backing']
    f_top = row['rehypothecation_factor']
    top_pen = row['liquidation_penalty']
    
    non_reh_pen = (1 - f_top) * top_pen
    M_top = w_top * f_top
    
    backing = df[df['underlying_symbol'] == row['final_collateral']].copy()
    if backing.empty:
        return top_pen

    backing['w_eff'] = M_top * (backing['relative_backing'] * backing['rehypothecation_factor'])
    pen_contrib = (backing['w_eff'] * backing['liquidation_penalty']).sum()
    
    return non_reh_pen + pen_contrib[/code]
The third stage is calculating the actual Risk Capital required based on the previously calculated variables.
The documents herein specify the process for calculating the Probability Of Default.

core_children.core_name: Probability Of Default -Calculation Of Probability Of Default
core_children.core_content:
The probability of default ([code]PD[/code]) is calculated based on the [code]effective_liquidation_ratio[/code], the [code]effective_collateralization_ratio[/code], the [code]effective_volatility[/code], and [code]T[/code] using the Python code specified below, which assumes that returns for the collateral are normally distributed.

[code]PD = norm.cdf(ln(effective_liquidation_ratio / effective_collateralization_ratio)
                / (effective_volatility * sqrt(T)))
[/code]
Here [code]norm.cdf[/code] is the cumulative distribution function of the standard normal distribution.
The documents herein specify the process for calculating the Loss Given Default.

core_children.core_name: Loss Given Default -Calculation Of Recovery Rate
core_children.core_content: For each underlying market and backing collateral, a Recovery Rate is first calculated. This is equal to the product of (1) the Debt Backed ([code]debt_backed[/code]), (2) one minus the Effective Liquidation Penalty ([code]effective_liquidation_penalty[/code]), and (3) the Liquidation Efficiency Factor ([code]liquidation_efficiency_factor[/code]), divided by the Effective Exposure [code]effective_exposure[/code].

For each underlying market and backing collateral, the Loss Given Default ([code]LGD[/code]) is then calculated as one minus the Recovery Rate, with the stipulation that the Loss Given Default cannot be less than zero.

An implementation of the calculation of the Loss Given Default in Python is shown below:

[code]LGD = max(
    1 - (
        debt_backed
        * effective_liquidation_ratio
        * (1 - effective_liquidation_penalty)
        * liquidation_efficiency_factor
        / effective_exposure
    ),
    0
)[/code]
The documents herein specify the process for calculating the Asset Correlation Coefficient.

The Asset Correlation Coefficient ([code]ACC[/code]) for each underlying market and collateral is calculated using the formula specified in the Python code below as a function of the Probability Of Default ([code]PD[/code]).

[code]X = (1 - exp(-50 * PD)) / (1 - exp(-50))
ACC = 0.12 * X + 0.24 * (1 - X)[/code]
The documents herein specify the process for calculating Credit Risk.

core_children.core_name: Calculation Of Shifted Probability Of Default
core_children.core_content:
The Shifted Probability Of Default ([code]shifted_PD[/code]) for an underlying market and collateral is calculated as a function of the Probability Of Default ([code]PD[/code]) and the Asset Correlation Coefficient ([code]ACC[/code]) using the Python code below. This estimates the probability of default under a “stressed” scenario.

[code]shifted_PD = norm.cdf(
    ( norm.ppf(PD) + sqrt(ACC) * norm.ppf(0.999) )
    / sqrt(1 - ACC)
)[/code]

The Credit Risk ([code]credit_risk[/code]) for an underlying market and collateral is calculated as the difference between the Shifted Probability Of Default ([code]shifted_PD[/code]) and the Probability Of Default ([code]PD[/code]), multiplied by the Loss Given Default ([code]LGD[/code]). This is shown in the Python code below.

[code]credit_risk = (shifted_PD - PD) * LGD[/code]
The documents herein specify the process for calculating Risk Weighted Assets.

core_children.core_name: Risk Weighted Assets -Calculation Of Weighted Credit Risk
core_children.core_content:
The Weighted Credit Risk ([code]weighted_credit_risk(u)[/code]) for each underlying market ([code]u[/code]) is calculated as the sum of the product of the Relative Backing ([code]relative_backing(i)[/code]) and the Credit Risk ([code]credit_risk(i)[/code]) for each collateral ([code]i[/code]) backing that market.

[code]weighted_credit_risk(u) = sum(relative_backing(i) * credit_risk(i)) for all collaterals i backing u[/code]

The Total Debt Backed ([code]total_debt_backed(u)[/code]) for each underlying market ([code]u[/code]) is calculated as the sum of the Debt Backed ([code]debt_backed(i)[/code]) for each collateral ([code]i[/code]) backing that market.

[code]total_debt_backed(u) = sum(debt_backed(i)) for all collaterals i backing u[/code]

The Risk Weighted Assets ([code]RWA(u)[/code]) for each market ([code]u[/code]) is calculated as the product of the Total Debt Backed ([code]total_debt_backed(u)[/code]) and the Weighted Credit Risk ([code]weighted_credit_risk(u)[/code]).

[code]RWA(u) = total_debt_backed(u) * weighted_credit_risk(u)[/code]

Finally, the Risk Capital ([code]FLC(u)[/code]) required for each market [code]u[/code] is the Risk Weighted Assets ([code]RWA(u)[/code]) multiplied by three times 2.75%, reflecting that 100% weight is being placed on Credit Risk and 0% weight on Market Risk and Liquidity Risk.

[code]FLC(u) = RWA(u) * (0.0275 * 3)
[/code]
The total Risk Capital required across all markets is simply the sum of the Risk Capital required for each market.

The file herein contains an initial implementation of the calculations for a lending market that can be used as a reference implementation. This implementation will be improved and optimized in future iterations of the risk framework.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define the implementation of the smart contract risk framework. This implementation will be specified in a future iteration of the Atlas.
The documents herein define the core risk capital parameters

core_children.core_content: This document specifies the FLC loss allocation parameters.

The Tip FLC amount is 10% of the Star Agent's Total First Loss Capital (IFLC + SEFLC + TEFLC).

For example, if a Star Agent has $20 million of Internal First Loss Capital and $20 million of Star External First Loss Capital, the Tip First Loss Capital would be $4 million.
This document specifies the values of the Risk Capital sourcing ratios defined in A.3.3.1.2.2 - Risk Capital Sourcing Ratios.

core_children.core_name: Senior Per First Loss (SPFL) Ratio Values
core_children.core_content:
The SPFL ratio varies by the FLC type used for enablement:

• Internal First Loss Capital SPFL: 1.00,
• EPI-acquired Star External First Loss Capital SPFL: 0.75,
• EPI-acquired Tokenized External First Loss Capital SPFL: 0.50,
• SPFL-acquired External First Loss Capital (SEFLC or TEFLC): zero (0).

For example, if a Star Agent has $20 million of Internal First Loss Capital, $10 million of Star External First Loss Capital, and $10 million of Tokenized External First Loss Capital, then it would be able to count or “enable” $32.5 million of Senior Capital towards its required Risk Capital.

The External Per Internal (EPI) ratio is 1.00. For example, if a Star Agent has $20 million of Internal First Loss Capital, the EPI ratio enables it to source $20 million of External First Loss Capital.

This EPI ratio governs the initial mechanism for sourcing EFLC (SEFLC + TEFLC) and is based purely on the Star's IFLC; Stars can source EFLC via this initial mechanism up to a maximum amount equal to their IFLC holdings. However, additional EFLC may potentially be acquired via the alternative use of SPFL capacity, as described in A.3.3 - Risk Capital - Conceptual Framework - Eligible Assets - Risk Capital Sourcing Ratios - Definition Of Ratios - Senior Per First Loss Ratio - Alternative Use Of SPFL Capacity To Source External First Loss Capital.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define the implementation of the admin risk framework. This implementation will be specified in a future iteration of the Atlas.
The documents herein define operational requirements and protocols for the implementation of the First Loss Capital system in the risk framework.

core_children.core_name: Star External FLC Rental System Implementation
core_children.core_content: The implementation of the SEFLC rental system is defined as a Sky Primitive, specifically, the First Loss Capital Rental Primitive. See A.2.3.9.2 -  First Loss Capital Rental Primitive.
This document will be further developed in a future iteration of the Atlas.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define the process for updating Risk Capital requirements.

core_children.core_content: The documents herein define the requirements for updating inputs to the calculation of First Loss Capital requirements.

Inputs into Risk Capital calculations that are based on market or other external variables should be updated in real time or immediately after the underlying data is updated, if the underlying data is only updated periodically.

core_children.core_name: Responsibility For Cost Of Real Time Updates
core_children.core_content: Star Agents may develop data feeds that update the Atlas with their data at their expense. Alternatively, the Operational Executor Agent that has an Executor Accord with the Star Agent may perform this as part of the services it provides to the Star Agent. In this case, the Operational Executor Agent may charge whatever fees for this service the parties mutually agree on.

core_children.core_name: Near Term Real Time Updates By BA Labs
core_children.core_content: In the near term, BA Labs will provide real time data regarding major lending markets. BA Labs will be compensated for this work as part of its existing relationship with Sky Core and Spark.

In the near term, Star Agents may update calculations that are based on market or other external variables less frequently than real time. If they do so, Operational GovOps must apply a buffer to each variable that is not updated in real time to account for the fact that the variable may change in a way that increases the Risk Capital requirements between update frequencies.
The documents herein define processes to maintain and ensure the integrity of data that is used as an input to Risk Capital calculations.

The Star Agent may assume responsibility for updating the inputs to the Risk Capital calculations itself, either directly or through one of its agents, or may contract with Operational GovOps to perform that function.
If Operational GovOps does not perform the data updates, it must verify their accuracy.

In the near term, Operational GovOps must reduce exposure to any Asset Allocation Conduit if the Star Agent responsible for that Conduit fails to provide the timely information necessary to calculate the required Risk Capital. In the future, a system of monetary penalties must be developed to compensate Sky for the risk posed by late data submission.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define operational requirements and protocols for the implementation of the Senior Risk Capital system in the risk framework.

core_children.core_name: Sourcing Of Internal Senior Risk Capital
core_children.core_content: ISRC is sourced from the various surplus buffers, which are themselves funded via the Treasury Management Function. See A.2.4.1.2 - Allocation Steps.

core_children.core_name: External Senior Risk Capital And srUSDS System
core_children.core_content: The documents herein define rules and infrastructure pertaining to the sourcing of External Senior Risk Capital (ESRC). ESRC is capital provided by external investors to be invested as Senior Risk Capital of Star Agents.

srUSDS is the tokenized form of External Senior Risk Capital. Users can deposit USDS into the srUSDS contract to provide External Senior Risk Capital to the SRC system, making it available for origination by Star Agents. See A.3.3.2.4.3 - Senior Risk Capital (SRC) Origination Process. srUSDS holders can potentially earn higher yields in exchange for the risk that the External Senior Risk Capital will suffer losses.

The srUSDS contract utilizes distinct queues for managing conversions. Users can add USDS to the deposit queue for conversion into srUSDS; or add srUSDS to the redemption queue for conversion back into USDS at any point during the month.

Assets placed in either queue can be withdrawn by the user at any time before the Monthly Settlement Cycle begins.

At the Monthly Settlement Cycle, all assets remaining in the queues are processed: queued USDS is converted into srUSDS, and queued srUSDS is converted into USDS based on the prevailing Conversion Rate. See A.3.3.2.4.2.3 - Conversion Rate.

The conversion rate represents the amount of USDS redeemable per unit of srUSDS. This rate increases or decreases over time, reflecting the net return earned by the ESRC pool. The rate is updated at the conclusion of each Monthly Settlement Cycle based on the performance of the ESRC pool during the preceding month, according to the following standard compounding formula:

[code]New srUSDS Conversion Rate = Previous Conversion Rate * (1 + Monthly srUSDS Yield)[/code]

The parameters of this formula are defined in the documents herein.

[code]Previous Conversion Rate[/code] is the conversion rate (USDS per srUSDS) that was in effect at the beginning of the concluded monthly cycle. The initial conversion rate between srUSDS and USDS is 1:1.

[code]Monthly srUSDS Yield[/code] represents the net percentage return earned solely by the ESRC pool during the concluded monthly cycle, distributable to srUSDS holders via the conversion rate adjustment. It directly reflects the risk-adjusted performance of the capital deployed and is calculated as:

[code][(Total Interest Paid by Stars on ESRC Portion - Losses Allocated to ESRC) / ESRC Principal at start of month - Sky Spread] * (1 - ESRC Earnings Fee)[/code]

The Sky Spread and ESRC Earnings Fee are defined in A.3.3.2.4.2.3.3.1 - Sky Spread and A.3.3.2.4.2.3.3.2 - ESRC Earnings Fee, respectively.
Sky earns revenue from facilitating ESRC from two sources. These sources are included in the formula and defined in the subdocuments below.

Sky takes a spread equal to the Sky Spread on all ESRC balances. The Sky Spread is the difference between (1) the rate at which Star Agents borrow from Sky and (2) the Sky Savings Rate plus the Accessibility Reward Fee.
Sky takes a 5% fee on the net interest earnings generated from the ESRC portion of originated SRC before these earnings are distributed to srUSDS holders via the conversion rate adjustment.

Star Agents and third-party partners that drive srUSDS usage are entitled to a srUSDS Accessibility Reward similar to the Accessibility Reward paid on USDS balances. The srUSDS Accessibility Reward Fee is the same as the Accessibility Reward Fee on USDS. See A.2.3.8.1 - Accessibility Reward Primitive.

This document defines the operational details of the recurring monthly process allowing Star Agents to originate Senior Risk Capital (Originated Senior Risk Capital or OSRC) from the Total Senior Risk Capital (TSRC) pool. The process involves determining the available TSRC supply, a bidding phase, an allocation auction, and settlement aligned with the Monthly Settlement Cycle.

The SRC origination process operates monthly, synchronized with the Monthly Settlement Cycle. The bidding window occurs during the current month for SRC that is usable in the next month; the bidding window closes before settlement processing begins. Additional operational details, including timelines, will be specified in a future iteration of the Atlas.

core_children.core_name: Available Total Senior Risk Capital Pool Determination
core_children.core_content: Total Senior Risk Capital (TSRC) is the amount of Senior Risk Capital that is available each Monthly Settlement Cycle for Stars to originate and thus turn into Originated Senior Risk Capital (OSRC) through the monthly origination process. TSRC is the sum of designated Internal Senior Risk Capital (ISRC) from Sky surplus buffers and available External Senior Risk Capital (ESRC) from the srUSDS contract. Prior to each monthly bidding window, the TSRC is calculated and publicly announced. Additional operational details will be defined in a future iteration of the Atlas.

Participating Star Agents submit bids via the Powerhouse interface during the defined bidding window. Each bid must specify: (1) The quantity of SRC the Star seeks to originate, and (2) The maximum price the Star is willing to pay (expressed in basis points above the Base Rate) for using the OSRC during the upcoming monthly period. Additional operational details will be specified in a future iteration of the Atlas.

Bids are ranked descending by price. TSRC is allocated sequentially to the highest bids until the pool is exhausted or all bids are filled. The price of the lowest successful bid that receives an allocation (even partial) sets the uniform clearing price for the month. All winning bidders pay this clearing price per unit of OSRC allocated, regardless of their original bid price. Additional operational details will be specified in a future iteration of the Atlas.

During the Monthly Settlement Cycle following allocation, the cost (Allocated OSRC Quantity * Clearing Price) is deducted from each winning Star Agent’s operational account. Concurrently, the allocated OSRC amount is credited to the Star Agent’s Risk Capital account as OSRC for the upcoming monthly period.

OSRC is valid only for the single monthly period following the settlement cycle in which it was originated. Renewal requires successful participation in the next monthly origination process; there is no automatic rollover.

core_children.core_name: Originated Senior Risk Capital (OSRC) Rental Implementation
core_children.core_content: The operational process for inter-Star Agent OSRC risk-capital rentals is defined herein. SEFLC rentals are facilitated through the First Loss Capital Rental Primitive. See A.2.3.9.2 -  First Loss Capital Rental Primitive.

A registered OSRC-rental Ecosystem Accord effectively transfers the claim on the specified amount of the lender’s OSRC to the borrower for the duration of the agreement. The borrowing Star Agent must possess sufficient First Loss Capital (IFLC, SEFLC, TEFLC) and corresponding Senior Per First Loss (SPFL) capacity to enable the rented OSRC, such that it counts towards their own Risk Capital requirements. Rented OSRC that is not successfully enabled by the borrower pursuant to the SPFL ratio does not contribute to their Risk Capital calculation.

Rental of Originated Senior Risk Capital (OSRC) requires a registered Ecosystem Accord specifying: Amount, Duration, and Price/Terms. As the system permits sub-renting (allowing a Star to rent out OSRC it has previously rented), the Accord must explicitly state if the borrower is granted this permission. In regards to Duration, the Ecosystem Accord must specify the rental period, which cannot extend beyond the end of the current Monthly Settlement Cycle. Stars may agree on any Duration within this limit.

Standardized Ecosystem Accord templates will be provided in a future iteration of the Atlas.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define how changes to the required amount of Risk Capital should be handled.

core_children.core_name: Updates Due To Changed Market Parameters
core_children.core_content: The documents herein define the process for handling changes to Risk Capital requirements due to changes in market or other external variables.

core_children.core_name: Updates Due To Changed Market Parameters -Immediate Update To Risk Capital Requirements For Changes In Market Or External Variables
core_children.core_content: To the extent that a market or external variable changes in a way that increases the required Risk Capital, the Star Agent is immediately responsible for supplying the additional Risk Capital and is subject to penalties for any period in which this Risk Capital is not provided. It is the responsibility of the Star Agent to maintain sufficient capital so that they remain well capitalized in the face of changes to market conditions.

core_children.core_name: Updates Due To Changed Methodology Or Governance Parameters
core_children.core_content: The documents herein define the process for adjusting Risk Capital requirements whenever Sky Governance modifies the underlying methodology/parameters governing FLC calculations, thereby altering the FLC each Star Agent must hold.

core_children.core_name: Phased Update To Risk Capital Requirements For Changes In Methodology Or Governance Parameters
core_children.core_content: To the extent that a change to the implementation of the Risk Capital framework or a parameter set by Sky Governance changes the Risk Capital requirements, the additional Risk Capital required will phase in over time. The Star Agent must provide 50% of the additional Risk Capital within seven (7) calendar days of the change, and the remaining 50% on a pro rata basis over the following twenty one (21) calendar days.

core_children.core_name: No Penalties For Changes In Methodology Or Governance Parameters In Near Term
core_children.core_content: In the near term as the Risk Capital implementation continues to be developed, Star Agents will not be subject to penalties for failing to supply the incremental capital required by changes to the Risk Capital implementation. However, Operational GovOps must reduce the exposure to Allocation Conduits managed by the Star Agent to bring it back into compliance with capital requirements if the Star Agent does not work in good faith to provide the additional capital on the timeline specified above.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define monitoring and penalty mechanisms.

core_children.core_name: Monitoring As Part Of Settlement Cycle
core_children.core_content: Core GovOps reviews the calculation of Risk Capital by each Star Agent as part of the Settlement Cycle. In the event that it detects that Risk Capital requirements were violated, it applies the penalty mechanisms specified in A.3.3.2.7.2 - Penalty Mechanisms.

The documents herein specify the penalty mechanisms that exist to incentivize Star Agents and Operational Agents to ensure that sufficient capital is always held against investments by Star Agents and to protect Sky from risks due to insufficient capital.
The documents herein specify the penalty mechanisms for Star Agents for breaches of the Risk Capital requirements

core_children.core_name: Financial Penalties For Breach Of Capital Requirements
core_children.core_content: Core GovOps may impose a financial penalty on the Star Agent for failing to satisfy the Risk Capital requirements. In the near term, Core GovOps may determine a reasonable penalty taking into account (1) the size of the capital shortfall, (2) the length of its occurrence, (3) the ability of the Star Agent to pay, and (4) any other circumstances it deems relevant. Future iterations of the Risk Capital framework will specify a more detailed methodology for calculating penalties.

core_children.core_name: Additional Token Issuance For Breach Of Capital Requirements
core_children.core_content: In the event that financial penalties are not sufficient to address the shortfall because payment of the penalties would exacerbate an existing capital shortfall, Core GovOps may require the Star Agent to issue additional tokens and sell them to the extent it deems necessary. Operational GovOps will assist Core GovOps in executing any such transaction. Future iterations of the Risk Capital framework will specify a more detailed methodology for required token issuance.

core_children.core_name: Restrictions On Investments For Breach Of Capital Requirements
core_children.core_content: Core GovOps may impose whatever restrictions it deems necessary on the usage of the Allocation System Primitive by the Star Agent, including limiting the Star Agent’s exposure to certain Allocation Conduits or prohibiting the Star Agent from using certain Allocation Conduits. These restrictions may extend for a period of time that Core GovOps deems fit.

core_children.core_name: Conservatorship For Breach Of Capital Requirements
core_children.core_content: In the event that less extreme measures are not adequate to address the capital shortfall, Core GovOps may seek to put the Star Agent into conservatorship, in which case Sky Core Facilitators take direct control of the Star Agent to maximize value for Sky and other Star Agent stakeholders. Seeking to put a Star Agent into conservatorship requires immediate escalation to Sky Core Governance and requires an expedited Executive Vote as specified in A.1.8.1.5.4.1 - Known And Uncontentious Remedies.

core_children.core_name: Penalty Mechanisms For Operational Executor Agents
core_children.core_content: The documents herein specify the penalty mechanisms for Operational Executor Agents for breaches of Risk Capital requirements.

core_children.core_name: Financial Penalties For Operational Executor Agents
core_children.core_content: In the near term there are no penalties for Operational Executor Agents for failure of the Star Agents they supervise to maintain appropriate levels of capital. In future iterations of the Risk Framework, Operational Executor Agents will be required to put up collateral and act as “insurers” of all activities by the Star Agents they supervise with regard to the Sky Primitives. At that point Core GovOps will be able to seize this collateral in the event of failure to obtain compensation for breaches of the Risk Capital requirement by the Star Agent.

In the event that Core GovOps determines that the Operational Executor Agent is not appropriately supervising the activities of the Star Agent, Core GovOps may terminate the respective Executor Accord. This requires an immediate escalation to Sky Governance and requires an expedited Executive Vote as specified in A.1.8.1.5.4.1 - Known And Uncontentious Remedies. Core GovOps must arrange for another Operational Executor Agent to stand in until the Star Agent can Invoke the Executor Accord Primitive to enter into a new Executor Accord. The Star Agent must suspend all operations until a new Executor Accord is voted on and formally integrated into the Agent’s Artifact.
The documents herein specify measures for escalating situations directly to Sky Governance in the event that they cannot be resolved by Core GovOps.

In the event of a disagreement regarding the penalties for a violation of Risk Capital requirements, either party may escalate the matter to Sky Governance. In addition, the extraordinary remedies of Star Agent Conservatorship and Executor Accord Termination must always be escalated to a Sky Governance Executive Vote.

Once a matter has been escalated to Sky Governance, the Sky Core Facilitators may request any information they deem necessary from Core GovOps, Operational GovOps, and the Star Agent. Sky Governance then acts through a Governance Poll to determine its resolution of the matter.

scope_articles.article_content: Star Agents who invest capital from Sky’s Collateral Portfolio using the Allocation System Primitive must hold Risk Capital to protect Sky from potential losses on these investments. This Article sets forth the framework governing Risk Capital.

article_sections.section_content: This Section defines the conceptual framework for Risk Capital.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define the conceptual framework for determining the Aggregate Required Risk Capital (RRC) that Star Agents are required to hold to protect Sky from risks on their investments.

core_children.core_content: The Aggregate RRC that a Star Agent must maintain is equal to the sum of the Instance Total RRC for each Active Instance of the Allocation System Primitive that the Star Agent has deployed.

The Instance Total RRC for an Instance is equal to the sum of the Instance Financial RRC, Instance Smart Contract Risk RRC, and Instance Admin Risk RRC for that Instance. See A.3.3.1.1.3 - Instance Financial RRC, A.3.3.1.1.4 - Instance Smart Contract RRC, and A.3.3.1.1.5 - Instance Admin RRC.

Investments in Real World Assets, as defined in A.3.3.1.1.3.3.2.4 - Real World Assets, automatically have Instance Smart Contract RRC and Instance Admin RRC of zero. An additional type of Legal RRC, reflecting the unique risks of these assets, will be incorporated in future iterations of the risk framework.

core_children.core_name: Incorporation Of Additional Types Of RRC
core_children.core_content: Additional types of RRC reflecting other risk factors, including legal risk and oracle risk, will be incorporated in future iterations of the risk framework.

The documents herein define the conceptual framework for determining the Instance Financial RRC required to protect Sky from financial risk arising from an Instance of the Allocation System Primitive.

The conceptual framework for financial risk is designed to ensure that Star Agents hold sufficient capital to cover potential losses on their investments in case of an extreme market event. The financial risk framework is based on the Basel Accords developed by the Committee on Bank Supervision, including Basel II, Basel III, and Basel IV. These frameworks are then adapted to reflect the differences between traditional finance and decentralized finance.

Financial risk can be divided into three categories, as specified in the documents herein. These categories of risk are interrelated, so Instance Financial RRC is not directly associated with these risk categories. Instead, risk models are developed for different asset classes, as specified in A.3.3.1.1.3.3 - Financial Risk Models. The development of each of these risk models is informed by considerations regarding how each of the financial risk categories impacts the specific asset.
Credit risk is the risk that a loan or other debt instrument will not be repaid according to agreed terms.
Market risk is the risk that a financial asset will decline in value due to market conditions or changes in investor perceptions of the asset.
Liquidity risk is the risk that an asset cannot be sold quickly enough, or in sufficient quantity, at its fair market value without causing a significant price impact.

The conceptual framework for financial risk is implemented through asset class specific risk models. The documents herein define the asset classes that risk models exist or are being developed for, including the high level approach and examples of relevant assets. Investments may only be made using the Allocation System if they are covered by one of the Financial Risk models herein.
The documents herein define fully implemented asset-class-specific risk models.

The model for lending markets focuses on Credit Risk by incorporating asset volatility and liquidity factors. This model can be applied to lending markets such as SparkLend, Aave, Morpho, Fluid, and Maple. The implementation of the model for lending markets is specified in A.3.3.2.1.1.1.1 - Lending Markets.
The documents herein define pending asset-class-specific risk models along with near-term Instance Financial RRC Ratios to be used until the models are fully implemented.

The model for perpetual positions uses a modified Credit Risk model to evaluate the risk of tokenized delta-neutral positions, such as Ethena and Resolv. The implementation of the model for perpetual positions will be specified in a future iteration of the Atlas.

In the near term pending development of the implementation of the model for perpetual positions, the following Instance Financial RRC Ratios will apply:

• Direct Ethena exposure - 5% Instance Financial RRC Ratio
• Indirect Ethena exposure through Morpho - 3% Instance Financial RRC Ratio
• All other protocols - 4% Instance Financial RRC Ratio

The model for direct exposures combines Market Risk and Liquidity Risk to evaluate the risk of direct asset holdings of volatile cryptoassets, such as ETH, stETH, and WBTC. The implementation of the model for direct exposures will be specified in a future iteration of the Atlas.

In the near term pending development of the implementation of the model for direct exposures, a 25% Instance Financial RRC Ratio will be applied to all direct exposures. In the near term, direct exposures will be considered to be any assets held idle in a wallet controlled by a Star Agent.

The model for bond-like instruments uses a modified Credit Risk model to evaluate the risk of cryptoassets with duration risk, such as Pendle’s PT-tokens. The implementation of the model for bond-like instruments will be specified in a future iteration of the Atlas.

In the near term pending development of the implementation of the model for bond-like exposures, a 4% Instance Financial RRC Ratio will be applied to all bond-like exposures. In the near term, only Pendle PT tokens will be eligible for investment under this provision.

The model for Real World Assets is based on direct application of existing frameworks to evaluate the risk of tokenized Real World Assets, such as BUIDL, JTRSY, and USTB. The implementation of the model for Real World Assets will be specified in a future iteration of the Atlas.

In the near term pending development of the implementation of the model for Real World Assets, a 0% Instance Financial RRC Ratio will be applied to Real World Assets. In the near term, only BUIDL, JTRSY, and USTB will be eligible for investment under this provision.

The model for Cash Stablecoins is based on identifying stablecoins, such as USDC and USDT, that do not present significant Market Risk, Credit Risk, or Liquidity Risk. The implementation of the model for Cash Stablecoins will be specified in a future iteration of the Atlas.

In the near term pending development of the implementation of the model for Cash Stablecoins, a 0% Instance Financial RRC will be applied to Cash Stablecoins. In the near term, only USDS, sUSDS, Dai, sDai, USDC, and USDT (either held directly or through decentralized exchanges such as Curve) will be eligible for investment under this provision.

The documents herein define the conceptual framework for determining the Instance Smart Contract RRC required to protect Sky from smart contract risk arising from an Instance of the Allocation System Primitive.

Smart Contract Risk refers to the risk that an investment through the Allocation System will experience a loss of funds due to a bug or technical exploit. This risk may arise from the protocol invested in or other aspects of the investment such as bridging requirements.

The determination of Smart Contract Risk should initially be based on an evaluation of the risk that the investment could experience losses due to a bug or technical exploit. This determination should take into account factors such as (1) the technical complexity of the code base (e.g. cyclomatic complexity, decision points, number of external calls, lines of code), (2) the number, quality, and recency of audits, and (3) the “Lindiness” of the relevant smart contracts.

The risk of a technical exploit in one protocol is unlikely to be correlated with the risk of a technical exploit in an unrelated protocol. Therefore, the risks from small exposures to even relatively risky protocols are likely to be diversified away. In contrast, very large exposures to even relatively safe protocols can create a risk to a Star Agent and potentially to Sky itself if there is a technical exploit, especially if multiple Star Agents have the same exposure. To account for this, the calculation of Instance Smart Contract RRC should take into account the risk rating, the level of exposure of the Star, and the level of exposure of the Sky Ecosystem.
The implementation of the calculation of Instance Smart Contract RRC is specified in A.3.3.2.1.2 - Instance Smart Contract RRC Implementation.
The documents herein define the conceptual framework for determining the Instance Admin RRC required to protect Sky from admin risk arising from an Instance of the Allocation System Primitive.

Admin Risk refers to the risk that an investment through the Allocation System will experience a loss of funds due to abuse of privileged access to a protocol. This risk may arise from a multisig or another actor that has the ability to modify the protocol or access funds without going through a governance process.

The determination of Admin Risk should initially be based on an evaluation of the risk that an investment could experience losses due to an abuse of privileged access. This determination should take into account factors such as (1) the level of privileged access that exists, (2) the security delay that such access is subject to, and (3) the “Lindiness” of the system.

The risk of an abuse of privileged access in one protocol is unlikely to be correlated with the risk of an abuse of privileged access in an unrelated protocol. Therefore, the risks from small exposures to even relatively risky protocols are likely to be diversified away. In contrast, very large exposures to even relatively safe protocols can create a risk to a Star Agent and potentially to Sky itself if there is an abuse of privileged access, especially if multiple Star Agents have the same exposure. To account for this, the calculation of Instance Admin RRC should take into account the risk rating, the level of exposure of the Star, and the level of exposure of the Sky Ecosystem.
The implementation of the calculation of Instance Admin RRC is specified in A.3.3.2.1.3 - Instance Administrative RRC Calculation.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define the conceptual framework for types of capital that contribute to a Star Agent’s Total Risk Capital (TRC). Star Agents must maintain at all times a level of TRC that exceeds their Aggregate RRC or be subject to penalties.

core_children.core_content: Total Risk Capital is capital that is currently eligible, available, and verifiably under the Star's control. For capital to be included in a Star Agent's TRC, it must be currently deployable towards covering its Required Risk Capital obligations. Consequently, in-flight capital (e.g., assets being bridged) does not contribute towards a Star’s TRC. Similarly, commitments for future capital, such as Ecosystem Accords for renting Star-External Junior Risk Capital (SEJRC) or Tokenized External Junior Risk Capital (TEJRC) that have been agreed upon by counterparties but are not yet formally codified within the Atlas, cannot contribute towards a Star’s TRC until such formalization is complete.
TRC comprises two categories based on loss absorption seniority: Junior Risk Capital (JRC) and Senior Risk Capital (SRC), further divided by source.

Junior Risk Capital (JRC) is the first capital to absorb losses on investments under the Allocation System. Junior Risk Capital must experience 100% losses before any losses are absorbed by Senior Risk Capital.
The documents herein define the types of Junior Risk Capital.

Internal Junior Risk Capital is capital owned by the Star Agent itself. It serves as the foundation for the Star's risk capacity: the amount of IJRC directly dictates the maximum External JRC the Star can source (governed by the External Per Internal ratio) and provides the primary capacity for enabling Senior Risk Capital (via the Senior Per Junior ratio), while also bearing the initial impact of any losses (the Tip JRC mechanism). IJRC must be eligible assets held in the Star Agent’s designated treasury account (e.g., SubProxy), controlled by the Star’s root governance.

See A.3.3.1.2.3.1.1 - External Per Internal Ratio, A.3.3.1.2.3.1.2 - Senior Per Junior Ratio, and A.3.3.1.2.2.1.2 - JRC Loss Allocation Rules.

core_children.core_name: Types Of Eligible Assets For IJRC
core_children.core_content:
