The documents herein define the types of assets that qualify as Internal Junior Risk Capital.

These types will be specified in a future iteration of the Risk Framework.

SEJRC is JRC rented from another Star Agent via the A.2.3.9.2 - Junior Risk  Capital Rental Primitive. The capital remains in the lender's treasury but counts towards the borrower's JRC amount. SEJRC is represented by a registered Ecosystem Accord signifying that a specified amount of the lending Agent's JRC (held within the lender's treasury) is designated to count towards the borrowing Agent's JRC requirement for the duration and terms specified in the Accord.

Tokenized External Junior Risk Capital is capital provided by external capital providers depositing sUSDS into the Tokenized External Junior Risk Capital smart contract, from which Stars can encumber funds. The details of this contract will be specified in a future iteration of the Atlas.
This document defines the conceptual framework for distributing losses within the JRC layer.

The Tip JRC mechanism dictates that losses from a distinct risk event are first absorbed entirely by the Star Agent's Internal Junior Risk Capital (IJRC), up to a specific threshold amount. This threshold amount is calculated as a percentage of Total JRC and is specified in A.3.3.2.2.1 - JRC Loss Allocation Parameters.

Only losses exceeding this threshold amount trigger absorption by other Junior Risk Capital components - specifically, the remaining IJRC and External Junior Risk Capital. This initial absorption solely by IJRC ensures that Star Agents are highly incentivized to prevent losses and avoids the need to resolve disputes regarding the allocation of small losses to EJRC providers.

If a loss event exceeds the Tip JRC amount, the remaining loss is allocated pro-rata across the rest of the JRC capital. This includes the remaining Internal Junior Risk Capital (Total IJRC minus the Tip amount) and all External Junior Risk Capital (Star External Junior Risk Capital plus Tokenized External Junior Risk Capital).

The JRC loss allocation rules are applied independently based on the total losses attributed to each distinct risk event. The concept of “per-event basis” is crucial; the classification of a distinct risk event (classifying losses as one large event versus multiple smaller ones) can dictate whether the Tip JRC threshold is breached.

Breaching the threshold activates the pro-rata sharing mechanism for any losses exceeding the Tip JRC amount, distributing such excess losses between both the remaining Internal JRC (IJRC) and all External JRC (EJRC). Conversely, if the threshold is not breached for an event, the loss is contained entirely within the Tip layer and absorbed solely by IJRC.

The operational process for the determination of distinct risk events will be defined in a future iteration of the Atlas.

Senior Risk Capital (SRC) is protected from losses relative to Junior Risk Capital. Senior Risk Capital only begins to absorb losses after Junior Risk Capital has experienced 100% losses and absorbs 100% of losses thereafter.

Senior Risk Capital can be originated from Sky Core on a monthly basis as part of the Monthly Settlement Cycle; Star Agents can also rent Senior Risk Capital from each other.
The documents herein define the types of Senior Risk Capital.

Internal Senior Risk Capital (ISRC) is capital provided from Sky Core’s Surplus Buffer. The composition of Internal Senior Risk Capital will be specified in more detail in a future iteration of the Atlas.

External Senior Risk Capital (ESRC) is capital provided from the srUSDS smart contract, which allow users to provide USDS to Sky Core to serve as senior risk capital in exchange for higher returns. The mechanics of this are specified in A.3.3.2.4.2 - External Senior Risk Capital And srUSDS System.

The documents herein define the required relationship and constraints between the different types of Total Risk Capital. These ensure that the Star Agent maintains the appropriate level of Internal Junior Risk Capital relative to its External and Senior capital exposures, ensuring sufficient “skin in the game” with respect to its investments.
The documents herein define the Total Risk Capital sourcing ratios.

The External Per Internal (EPI) ratio determines the maximum amount of External Junior Risk Capital (SEJRC and TEJRC combined) that a Star Agent can source directly based on its held Internal Junior Risk Capital (IJRC). This acts as the primary constraint ensuring a baseline level of the Star's own capital backs its external JRC sourcing. The required External Per Internal ratio is specified in A.3.3.2.2.2.1 - External Per Internal (EPI) Ratio Value.

The Senior Per Junior (SPJ) ratio defines the effectiveness of each type of Junior Risk Capital (IJRC, SEJRC, TEJRC) in supporting Senior Risk Capital (SRC). Specifically, it quantifies how many units of SRC (whether Originated or Rented) can be activated or "enabled" per unit of a given JRC type.

A Star Agent's overall “SPJ capacity” is calculated by summing the potential enablement contributed by each type of JRC it holds (IJRC, SEJRC or TEJRC), where each contribution equals the amount of that JRC type multiplied by its specific SPJ ratio.

Since SRC only absorbs losses after JRC is depleted,  the SPJ enablement mechanism validates that sufficient JRC backing exists, allowing enabled SRC to be counted as part of the Star Agent's total eligible Risk Capital. Therefore, a Star Agent might hold a certain amount of OSRC or rented SRC, but only the portion that is successfully "enabled" by its JRC according to the SPJ ratios is accounted by the system as eligible capital contributing towards the Star's satisfaction of its Required Risk Capital. The required ratio of Senior Per Junior is specified in A.3.3.2.2.2.2 - Senior Per Junior (SPJ) Ratio Values.

core_children.core_name: Alternative Use Of SPJ Capacity To Source External Junior Risk Capital
core_children.core_content:
A Star Agent may optionally choose to deploy all or some portion of its calculated SPJ capacity not to enable Senior Risk Capital, but instead to source additional External Junior Risk Capital. This provides a secondary mechanism to increase the total JRC potentially beyond the amount initially constrained by the EPI ratio applied solely to IJRC.

Crucially, the EJRC tranche acquired through utilizing SPJ capacity is permanently accounted for as having an SPJ ratio of zero (0). It contributes to the JRC buffer but provides no capacity itself for enabling SRC in any subsequent calculations. This contrasts with EJRC sourced normally via the EPI ratio, which retains its standard SPJ capacity.

core_children.core_name: Interaction Between Ratios And SPJ Capacity
core_children.core_content:
The sourcing of External Junior Risk Capital and enablement of Senior Risk Capital involves a sequential application of the Risk Capital sourcing ratios in conjunction with the Star Agent’s deployment of its SPJ capacity.

First, the EPI ratio sets the initial limit for sourcing standard, SPJ-bearing EJRC based solely on the Star Agent’s IJRC.
Second, the total SPJ capacity is calculated from the Star Agent’s resulting JRC pool (IJRC + any EPI-sourced EJRC).

Finally, this total calculated SPJ capacity can be allocated by the Star Agent to enable SRC (standard use), and/or to source additional, non-SPJ-bearing EJRC using the mechanism described under A.3.3.1.2.3.1.2.1 -  Alternative Use Of SPJ Capacity To Source External Junior Risk Capital. Any specific portion of the total SPJ capacity allocated towards sourcing EJRC cannot simultaneously be allocated towards enabling SRC.

article_sections.section_content: This Section defines the current implementation of the Risk Capital framework.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define the implementation of the risk framework for calculating required Total Instance RRC.

core_children.core_content: The documents herein define the implementation of the risk framework for calculating Instance Financial RRC.

The documents herein define the calculation of Instance Financial RRC for specific asset classes. In the near term, the only implementation that is defined is for lending markets. In the future additional implementations will be added herein.
The documents herein define the calculation of Instance Financial RRC for lending markets.

core_children.core_name: Process For Calculating Instance Financial RRC
core_children.core_content: The documents herein define the process for calculating Instance Financial RRC for lending markets.

The first step is calculating the Probability Of Default $PD$. $PD$ is calculated using the following formula:

$$
\text{PD} = N(-d_1) + N(-d_2) \left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n \text{LT}i V_0^i}{\sum{j=1}^m D_0^j} \right)^{-2a}
$$

Here $N$ is the normal cumulative probability distribution function.

The parameters of this formula are specified in the subdocuments herein.

core_children.core_name: Leverage Adjusted Drift To Risk Ratio
core_children.core_content:
The Leverage Adjusted Drift To Risk Ratio $a$ is calculated as follows:

$$
a = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n w_U^i (r_c^i + r_s^i) - \sum_{j=1}^m w_D^j (r_d^j + r_s^j) + \rho_{UD} \sigma_U \sigma_D - \sigma_U^2}{\sigma_U^2 + \sigma_D^2 - 2 \rho_{UD} \sigma_U \sigma_D}
$$

The Distance To Default $d_1$ is calculated using the following formula:

$$
d_1 = \frac{\ln\left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n \text{LT}i V_0^i}{\sum{j=1}^m D_0^j} \right) + \left( \sum_{i=1}^n w_U^i (r_c^i + r_s^i) - \sum_{j=1}^m w_D^j (r_d^j + r_s^j) + \rho_{UD} \sigma_U \sigma_D - \sigma_U^2 \right) T}{\sqrt{\sigma_U^2 + \sigma_D^2 - 2 \rho_{UD} \sigma_U \sigma_D} \sqrt{T}}
$$

The Downward Adjusted Distance To Default $d_2$ is calculated using the following formula:

$$
d_2 = \frac{\ln\left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n \text{LT}i V_0^i}{\sum{j=1}^m D_0^j} \right) - \left( \sum_{i=1}^n w_U^i (r_c^i + r_s^i) - \sum_{j=1}^m w_D^j (r_d^j + r_s^j)+ \rho_{UD} \sigma_U \sigma_D - \sigma_U^2 \right) T}{\sqrt{\sigma_U^2 + \sigma_D^2 - 2 \rho_{UD} \sigma_U \sigma_D} \sqrt{T}}
$$

core_children.core_name: Total Variance Of Underlying Asset Portfolio
core_children.core_content:
The Total Variance Of Underlying Asset Portfolio $\sigma_U^2$ is calculated using the following formula:

$$
\sigma_U^2 = \sum_{i,k=1}^n w_U^i w_U^k \sigma_V^i \sigma_V^k \rho_{V,ik}
$$

The Total Variance Of Debt Portfolio $\sigma_D^2$ is calculated using the following formula:

$$
\sigma_D^2 = \sum_{j,l=1}^m w_D^j w_D^l \sigma_D^j \sigma_D^l \rho_{D,jl}
$$

core_children.core_name: Correlation Between Asset Portfolio And Debt Portfolio
core_children.core_content:
The Correlation Between Asset Portfolio And Debt Portfolio $\rho_{UD}$ is calculated using the following formula:

$$
\rho_{UD} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^m w_U^i w_D^j \sigma_V^i \sigma_D^j \rho_{VD,ij}}{\sqrt{\sigma_U^2 \sigma_D^2}}
$$

core_children.core_name: Weight Of Asset In Underlying Asset Portfolio
core_children.core_content:
The weight of asset $i$ in the underlying asset portfolio $w_U^i$ is calculated using the following formula:

$$
w_U^i = \frac{\text{LT}i V_0^i}{\sum{k=1}^n \text{LT}_k V_0^k}
$$

core_children.core_name: Weight Of Debt Instrument In Debt Portfolio
core_children.core_content:
The weight of debt instrument $j$ in the debt portfolio $w_D^j$ is calculated using the following formula:

$$
w_D^j = \frac{D_0^j}{\sum_{l=1}^m D_0^l}
$$
The Return On Asset $r_c^i$ of an asset $i$ is the yield earned for supplying the asset in the lending market.

The Asset Yield $r_s^i$ of an asset $i$ is the income yield of the asset and would include any dividends or interest paid by the asset and/or asset issuer, including staking rewards for yield-bearing assets.
The Cost Of Debt $r_d^j$ of a debt instrument $j$ is the interest rate on the debt.

The Debt Yield $r_s^j$ of a debt instrument $j$ is the income yield on the debt asset. It includes any dividends or interest paid by the debt asset and/or debt asset issuer, including staking rewards for yield-bearing debt assets.

The correlation coefficient $\rho$ between two instruments is the correlation of block-weighted log returns of those assets over the last 365 days. In the documents herein, the correlation coefficient is followed by subscripts indicating the relevant instruments. For example, $\rho_{UD}$ is the correlation between the underlying asset portfolio $U$ and the debt portfolio $D$ as specified in A.3.3.2.1.1.1.1.1.1.6 - Correlation Between Asset Portfolio And Debt Portfolio. The subscript may begin with $V$ or $D$ to indicate whether the relevant instruments are part of the asset portfolio or debt portfolio. For example, $\rho_{VD,ij}$ is the correlation coefficient between the asset $i$ and the debt instrument $j$.

The Loan To Value Ratio $LT_i$ of an asset $i$ is the ratio of the debt backed by an asset to the value of the asset backing it:

$$
LT_i = \frac{D_0^i}{V_0^i}
$$
The Asset Value $V_0^i$ of an asset $i$ is the market value of that asset.
The Debt Value $D_0^i$ of a debt instrument is the notional value of the debt.
The Time Horizon $T$ is the time horizon in years over which the Probability Of Default is being estimated. The value of the $T$ parameter is [code]1[/code].

The second step is calculating the Loss Given Default $LGD$. $LGD$ is calculated using the following formula:

$$
LGD = min(1 - \frac{(1 - LP) * (1 - S)}{LT}, 0)
$$

Here $min$ is the mathematical minimum function that returns the lower of the two specified parameters.

The parameters of this formula are specified in the subdocuments herein. All of these parameters should be specified as decimal numbers. For example, 3% should be specified as [code]0.03[/code].
The Liquidation Penalty $LP$ is the contractually agreed upon liquidation penalty if the asset is liquidated to satisfy the debt.
The Slippage $S$ is the estimated slippage for liquidating the entire position in one block.
The Liquidation Threshold $LT$ is the value of the debt as a percentage of the collateral value at which the lender may liquidate the collateral to satisfy the debt.

The third step is to calculate the Asset Correlation Coefficient $R$. $R$ is calculated as follows:

$$
R = a \times \left(1 - e^{-K \times PD}\right) + b \times \left(1 - \left(1 - e^{-K \times PD}\right)\right)
$$

Here $e$ is the base of the natural logarithm.

The parameters of this formula are specified in the subdocuments herein.
The Lower Bound $a$ is an estimate of the correlation between assets during “calm” periods. It is set of [code]0.13[/code].
The Upper Bound $b$ is an estimate of the correlation between assets during “stressful” market environments. It is set to [code]0.33[/code].
The Sensitivity Factor [code]K[/code] is a tuning parameter indicating how quickly the correlations transition between $a$ and $b$. It is set to [code]10[/code].

The fourth step is to calculate the Capital Requirement Without Buffers $K$. $K$ is calculated as follows:

$$
K = \left[ LGD \times N\left( \frac{N^{-1}(PD) + \sqrt{R} \cdot N^{-1}(0.999)}{\sqrt{1-R}} \right) - PD \times LGD \right]
$$

Here $N$ is the cumulative normal probability distribution function and $N^{-1}$ is the inverse cumulative normal probability distribution function.

The final step is to calculate the Instance Financial RRC $RRC$. $RRC$ is calculated as follows:

$$
\text{RRC} = K \times \frac{1}{CR} \times \text{EAD} \times \text{ECR}
$$

The parameters of this formula are specified in the subdocuments herein.
The Capital Ratio $CR$ is the capital ratio without additional buffers. It is set to [code]8.75%[/code].
The Exposure At Default $EAD$ is the total amount of funds from the Allocation System that have been deployed into the decentralized lending protocol.

The Effective Capital Ratio $ECR$ is the capital ratio included additional capital buffers established by Sky Governance as part of the Risk Framework. There are currently no additional capital buffers so the $ECR$ is equal to the $CR$, which is [code]8.75%[/code].

The calculation of Instance Financial RRC for lending markets is applicable to the following protocols:

• Aave v2
• Aave v3
• SparkLend
• Morpho
• Maple

The application by a Star Agent of the risk framework for lending markets to any protocols not listed above must be approved by its Operational Executor Agent. The Operational Executor shall consult with the Stability Scope Advisors and the Stability Facilitators in making this determination. Any such approval must be posted to the Sky Forum under the tag for the Star Agent.
The documents herein define exceptions to the calculation of Instance Financial RRC for specific lending protocols.

Because Maple accepts Native BTC and Native SOL as collateral, alternative Slippage parameters should be used for these collaterals. See A.3.3.2.1.1.1.1.1.2.2 - Slippage. The alternative Slippage parameters to be used are specified in the documents herein.

The Slippage for a Native BTC exposure is calculated as half the Slippage for an ETH exposure of the same USD amount:

$$
\text{slippage}{btc} = \frac{\text{slippage}{eth} }{2}
$$

For example, if the Slippage for a $40M ETH exposure is 2% then the Slippage for a $40M Native BTC exposure would be 1%.

The Slippage for a Native SOL exposure is calculated as double the Slippage for an ETH exposure of the same USD amount:

$$
\text{slippage}{sol} = 2 \times \text{slippage}{eth}
$$

For example, if the Slippage for a $40M ETH exposure is 2% then the Slippage for a $40M Native SOL exposure would be 4%.
The Instance Financial RRC Ratio for Fluid is 3%.

A reference implementation of the calculation of Instance Financial RRC for lending markets is included herein.

[code]import math
from collections import defaultdict
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import norm

# Constants
RISK_FREE_RATE = 0.04  # SOFR
TIME_HORIZON = 1       # 1 year

class FinancialRRCModel:
    def __init__(self):
        # For demo purposes, we hardcode a dummy correlation map
        self.token_correlation_map = {
            'TOKENA': {'TOKENB': 0.5},
            'TOKENB': {'TOKENA': 0.5},
        }

    def _calculate_effective_volatility(self, list_1, list_2):
        """
        Calculate the effective variance between two sets of positions.
        """
        effective_variance = 0.0
        for sym1, pos1 in list_1.items():
            for sym2, pos2 in list_2.items():
                if sym1 == sym2:
                    corr = 1.0
                else:
                    corr = self.token_correlation_map.get(sym1, {}).get(sym2, 
                           self.token_correlation_map.get(sym2, {}).get(sym1, 0.0))
                effective_variance += (
                    pos1["share"]
                    * pos2["share"]
                    * pos1["volatility_30d"]
                    * pos2["volatility_30d"]
                    * corr
                )
        return effective_variance

    def _estimate_rrc_for_position(self, wallet_data):
        """
        Estimate the required-risk capital (RRC) for a given wallet_data dict.
        """
        # 1) compute effective volatilities
        var_coll = self._calculate_effective_volatility(
            wallet_data["collateral_positions"],
            wallet_data["collateral_positions"],
        )
        var_debt = self._calculate_effective_volatility(
            wallet_data["debt_positions"],
            wallet_data["debt_positions"],
        )
        vol_coll = math.sqrt(var_coll)
        vol_debt = math.sqrt(var_debt)

        # 2) correlation collateral↔debt
        cov_cd = self._calculate_effective_volatility(
            wallet_data["collateral_positions"],
            wallet_data["debt_positions"],
        )
        corr_cd = cov_cd / (vol_coll * vol_debt) if vol_coll * vol_debt > 0 else 0.0

        # 3) drift terms
        eff_coll_rate = sum(p["share"] * p["supply_apy_30d"]
                            for p in wallet_data["collateral_positions"].values())
        eff_borrow_rate = sum(p["share"] * p["borrow_apy_30d"]
                              for p in wallet_data["debt_positions"].values())
        eff_stake_rate = sum(p["share"] * p["staking_apy_30d"]
                             for p in wallet_data["collateral_positions"].values())

        drift_cd = (
            eff_coll_rate + eff_stake_rate - eff_borrow_rate
            + (var_debt - var_coll) / 2
        )

        vol_cd = math.sqrt(
            max(var_coll + var_debt - 2 * corr_cd * vol_coll * vol_debt, 0)
        )

        # 4) Black-Cox inputs
        a = (drift_cd - vol_cd**2 / 2) / (vol_cd**2) if vol_cd > 0 else 0.0
        L = wallet_data["collateral_usd_lt"]
        D = wallet_data["debt_usd"]
        log_term = math.log(L / D) if L > 0 and D > 0 else float("-inf")
        denom = vol_cd * math.sqrt(TIME_HORIZON) if vol_cd > 0 else 1e-10

        d1 = (log_term + (drift_cd - vol_cd**2/2) * TIME_HORIZON) / denom
        d2 = (log_term - (drift_cd - vol_cd**2/2) * TIME_HORIZON) / denom

        # 5) Probability of Default
        try:
            pd = (
                norm.cdf(-d1)
                + norm.cdf(-d2) * (L / D) ** (-2 * a)
            )
        except OverflowError:
            pd = 1.0
        pd = max(0.0, min(pd, 1.0))

        # 6) Loss Given Default
        recovery = sum(
            p["share"] * (1 - p["liquidation_penalty"]) * (1 - p["slippage"]) 
            / p["liquidation_threshold"]
            for p in wallet_data["collateral_positions"].values()
        )
        lgd = max(0.0, min(1 - recovery, 1.0))

        # 7) Exposure at Default
        ead = sum(
            debt["debt_usd"] * math.exp((RISK_FREE_RATE + debt["borrow_apy_30d"]) * TIME_HORIZON)
            for debt in wallet_data["debt_positions"].values()
        )

        # 8) Asset Correlation Coefficient
        a_acc, b_acc, c_acc = 0.13, 10, 0.33
        exp_term = math.exp(-b_acc * pd)
        acc = a_acc * (1 - exp_term)/(1-math.exp(-b_acc)) + c_acc*(1 - (1-exp_term)/(1-math.exp(-b_acc)))

        # 9) Credit risk weight
        default_threshold = (
            norm.ppf(max(pd, 1e-10)) + math.sqrt(acc) * norm.ppf(0.999)
        ) / math.sqrt(1 - acc)
        credit_risk = max(lgd * norm.cdf(default_threshold) - lgd * pd, 0)

        # 10) RRC
        rrc = credit_risk * ead
        return rrc

# -------- DEMO USAGE --------

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # 1) Build a dummy wallet_data structure
    wallet_data = {
        "collateral_positions": {
            "TOKENA": {
                "share": 0.6,
                "volatility_30d": 0.2,
                "supply_apy_30d": 0.05,
                "staking_apy_30d": 0.02,
                "liquidation_penalty": 0.1,
                "liquidation_threshold": 0.8,
                "slippage": 0.01,
            },
            "TOKENB": {
                "share": 0.4,
                "volatility_30d": 0.25,
                "supply_apy_30d": 0.04,
                "staking_apy_30d": 0.015,
                "liquidation_penalty": 0.12,
                "liquidation_threshold": 0.85,
                "slippage": 0.015,
            },
        },
        "debt_positions": {
            "TOKENA": {
                "share": 0.7,
                "volatility_30d": 0.2,
                "borrow_apy_30d": 0.06,
                "debt_usd":  100_000,
            },
            "TOKENB": {
                "share": 0.3,
                "volatility_30d": 0.25,
                "borrow_apy_30d": 0.07,
                "debt_usd":  50_000,
            },
        },
        "collateral_usd_lt": 200_000,
        "debt_usd": 150_000,
    }

    # 2) Instantiate and run
    model = FinancialRRCModel()
    rrc = model._estimate_rrc_for_position(wallet_data)
    print(f"Estimated Required Risk Capital (RRC): ${rrc:,.2f}")
[/code]
The documents herein define the implementation of the risk framework for calculating Instance Smart Contract RRC.
The first step in calculating Instance Smart Contract RRC with respect to an Allocation System opportunity is to identify the set of relevant smart contracts for the opportunity.

First, all relevant smart contracts must be identified. Relevant smart contracts are those that are critical for the project being invested in, including core business logic, vaults, token contracts, and proxy implementations.

External sources such as third party data providers, security consultants, and project documentation may be used to help identify relevant smart contracts. However, the Star Agent deploying funds from the Collateral Portfolio into the opportunity retains overall responsibility for the reasonableness of identifying the relevant smart contracts.

Second, the source code for each relevant contract must be obtained. The source code should be obtained for the deployed version of the contract using an appropriate block explorer (e.g. Etherscan for Ethereum Mainnet or L2s). The source code should include any dependencies or inherited library contracts that are part of the live system.

The second step in calculating the Instance Smart Contract RRC with respect to an Allocation System opportunity is to calculate the Smart Contract Risk Rating $SCRR$ for the covered smart contracts identified in A.3.3.2.1.2.1 - Defining Relevant Smart Contracts.  The $SCRR$ is calculated as follows:

$$
\text{SCRR} = min[\text{CAP}, (\text{SR} + \text{CCR}) \times \text{LAF} \times {AAF}]
$$

Here $min$ is the mathematical minimum function that returns the lesser of the specified parameters.

The parameters of this formula are specified in the subdocuments herein.
The Smart Contract Risk Rating Cap $\text{CAP}$ is a temporary cap on the Smart Contract Risk Rating. The value of the $CAP$ is [code]30[/code].
The Starting Rate $SR$ is an arbitrary starting risk rating for protocols. The value of the $SR$ is [code]25[/code].

The Code Complexity Rate $CCR$ is a measure of the complexity of the code of the smart contracts used by the protocol. The $CCR$ is calculated as follows:

$$
CCR = \text{CCRMax} \times min(1, \frac{\text{RawCCR} + 1}{\text{CCRUpperBound} + 1})
$$

Here the $min$ function is the mathematical minimum function that returns the lesser of the specified parameters.

The parameters of this formula are specified in the subdocuments herein.
The Code Complexity Rating Maximum $\text{CCRMax}$ is the maximum Code Complexity Rating for a protocol. The $\text{CCRMax}$ is set to [code]75[/code].
The Code Complexity Rating Upper Bound $\text{CCRUpperBound}$ is an arbitrary factor to normalize the Raw Code Complexity Rating. The $\text{CCRUpperBound}$ is set to [code]8,500[/code].

The Raw Code Complexity Rating $\text{RawCCR}$ is an unnormalized measure of the complexity of the code of the smart contracts that implement the protocol. The $\text{RawCCR}$ is calculated as follows:

$$
\text{RawCCR}=(\text{TCC} \times \text{CCweight}) + (\text{TDP} \times \text{DPweight}) + (\text{TEC} \times \text{ECweight}) + (\text{ID} \times \text{IDweight}) + (\frac{\text{CS}}{\text{CSfactor}} \times \text{CSweight})
$$

The parameters of this formula are specified in the subdocuments herein.

Cyclomatic complexity measures the number of independent execution paths through a unit of code. The total cyclomatic complexity score is the sum of the cyclomatic complexity of each of the covered smart contracts.
The Cyclomatic Complexity Weight $\text{CCweight}$ is a weighting factor indicating the relative importance of the Total Cyclomatic Complexity versus other factors. It is set to [code]1[/code].

Decision points measure the number of branching points where conditional logic is applied. The Total Decision Points are the total number of Decision Points in all functions in the covered smart contracts.
The Decision Points Weight $\text{DPweight}$ is a weighting factor indicating the relative importance of the Total Decision Points versus other factors. It is set to [code]0.5[/code].
Total External Calls is the count of all external calls (e.g. [code]call[/code], [code]delegatecall[/code]) made in the covered smart contracts.
The External Calls Weight $\text{ECweight}$ is a weighting factor indicating the relative importance of the Total External Calls versus other factors. It is set to [code]1.5[/code].
Inheritance Depth is the maximum number of inheritance levels in any contract in the covered contracts.
The Inheritance Depth Weight $\text{IDweight}$ is a weighting factor indicating the relative importance of the Inheritance Depth versus other factors. It is set to [code]5[/code].
Code Size is the total number of lines of code in the covered contracts, excluding tests and documentation.
The Code Size Factory is an arbitrary factor to normalize the Code Size relative to other parameters. It is set to [code]1,000[/code].
The Code Size Weight $\text{CSweight}$ is a weighting factor indicating the relative importance of the Code Size versus other factors. It is set to [code]1[/code].

The Lindy Adjustment Factor $\text{LAF}$ is a measure of the “Lindiness” of the smart contracts and is based on the idea that vulnerable smart contracts with large TVL for a significant period of time would have already been hacked. Therefore, protocols with a greater time integrated TVL are safer, all other things equal, than protocols with a lower time integrated TVL. The $\text{LAF}$ is calculated as follows:

$$
\text{LAF} = max(0, 1 - \frac{ln(1 + \lambda \times \text{AGEeff})}{ln(1 + \lambda \times \text{max})})
$$

Here $max$ is the mathematical maximum function that returns the greater of the specified parameters and $ln$ is the natural logarithm.

The parameters of this formula are specified in the subdocuments herein.

The Decay Factor $lambda$ is a tuning parameter that represents an estimate of how quickly the risk of a set of smart contracts decreases as its effective age increases. The value of $lambda$ is set to [code]0.1[/code].
The Maximum Age $\text{max}$ is the effective age, in months, at which the risk of a set of smart contracts has decayed to zero. The value of $\text{max}$ is set to [code]60[/code].

The Effective Age $\text{AGEeff}$ is the age of the contracts adjusted for the TVL of the contracts. The $\text{AGEeff}$ is calculated as follows:

$$
\text{AGEeff}=\text{CA} \times ln(1 + \frac{\text{gmTVL}}{\text{TVLthreshold}})
$$

Here $ln$ is the natural logarithm.

The parameters of this formula are specified in the subdocuments herein.

The contract age $\text{CA}$ is the average age, in months, of each of the relevant contracts. The age of each relevant contract should be measured based on the time elapsed between the date the contract was deployed and the date of calculation.
The Geometric Mean Total Value Locked $\text{gmTVL}$ is the geometric mean of the daily TVL over the contract age.
The Total Value Locked Threshold $\text{TVLthreshold}$ is a factor used to normalize the Geometric mean Total Value Locked. The $\text{TVLthreshold}$ is set to [code]100,000,000[/code].

The Audit Adjustment Factor ${AAF}$ is a measure of the extent to which security audits reduce the risk of a set of smart contracts by identifying and remediating vulnerabilities before they can be exploited. The ${AAF}$ is calculated as follows:

$$
\text{AAF} = \text{AF} \times \text{BaseRisk}
$$

The parameters of this formula are specified in the subdocuments herein.
The Base Risk $\text{BaseRisk}$ is a base risk factor in the absence of any audits. The $\text{BaseRisk}$ is set to [code]70[/code].

The Audit Factor $AF$ is a measure of the extent to which the Base Risk is reduced by audits. The $AF$ is calculated as:

$$
AF = \Pi{[1 - \text{effAuditValue} \times \text{decayFactor}]}
$$

The audit from each audit firm with the highest product of Effective Audit Value and Delay factor should be included in this calculation.

The parameters of this formula are specified in the subdocuments herein.

The Effective Audit Value $\text{effAuditValue}$ measures the effectiveness of a single audit in reducing the risk of a set of smart contracts and is a function of the percent of the code covered by the audit and the reputation of the audit firm. The $\text{effAuditValue}$ is calculated as:

$$
\text{effAuditValue} = \text{effectivenessCoefficient} \times \text{coverage}
$$

The parameters of this formula are specified in the subdocuments herein.

The Coverage $\text{coverage}$ is a measure of the percent of a set of smart contracts that were covered in the scope of an audit. The $\text{coverage}$ is calculated as:

$$
\text{coverage} = \frac{\text{LinesOfCodeCovered}}{\text{TotalLinesOfCode}}
$$

The parameters of this formula are specified in the subdocuments herein.
The Lines Of Code Covered $\text{LinesOfCodeCovered}$ is the number of lines of code of the relevant contracts that were within the scope of the audit, excluding documentation and tests.
The Total Lines Of Code $\text{TotalLinesOfCode}$ is the total number of lines of code of the relevant contracts, excluding documentation and tests.
The Effectiveness Coefficient $\text{effectivenessCoefficient}$ is a measure of the effectiveness of the particular audit firm and is estimated based on the tier of the audit firm.
Audit firms are divided into two tiers: top-tier and mid-tier.
The Effectiveness Coefficient of a top-tier audit firm is set to [code]0.8[/code].
The Effectiveness Coefficient of a mid-tier audit firm is set to [code]0.5[/code].

The subdocuments herein categorize specific audit firms into the top-tier and mid-tier categories. Audits from firms other than the ones specified herein may not be included in the calculation of the Audit Adjustment Factor.

The Protocol Security Workstream Lead must review this categorization on a regular basis and update it as necessary.

The top-tier audit firms are:

• ChainSecurity
• OpenZeppelin
• ConsenSys Diligence

The mid-tier audit firms are:

• Quantstamp
• SlowMist
• PeckShield
• Halborn
• Certora
• Hunter Security
• Omniscia
• Spearbit
• yAudit
• MixBytes
• Cyfrin
• SECBIT Labs
• Bramah Systems
• Zokyo
• Guardian Audits
• Zellic
• Code4rena
• Pashov
• ABDK
• Decurity
• OtterSec
• WatchPug
• Sigma Prime
• TrailOfBits
• Cantina
• Oxorio
• Enigma Dark
• Runtime Verification
• Pessimistic
• Solidified
• StErMi
• Sherlock

The Decay Factor $decayFactor$ is a parameter indicating how rapidly the effectiveness of audits in reducing risk decreases over time. The $decayFactor$ is calculated as follows:

$$
decayFactor =\begin{cases}1 & \text{if } auditAge \leq 2 \\\frac{10 - auditAge}{8} & \text{if } 2 < auditAge < 10 \\0 & \text{if } auditAge \geq 10\end{cases}
$$

The parameter of this formula is specified in the subdocument herein.

The Audit Age $auditAge$ is the age, in years, of the audit. The age of the audit should be measured based on the time elapsed between the date the audit report was issued and the date of calculation.

core_children.core_name: Smart Contract Risk Required Risk Capital Calculation
core_children.core_content: The third step in calculating the Instance Smart Contract RRC with respect to an Allocation System opportunity is to calculate the Instance Smart Contract RRC as a function of the Smart Contract Risk Rating.

core_children.core_name: Calculation Of Instance Smart Contract RRC Percentage Based on Risk Category
core_children.core_content: The methodology used to calculate the Instance Smart Contract RRC as a percentage of the exposure depends on the Risk Rating $r$ of the protocol as specified in the documents herein.
Low risk protocols are ones with a Risk Rating between less than or equal to [code]25[/code]. The methodology for low risk protocols is specified in the documents herein.
For low risk protocols, the F1 parameter $f_{1}$ should first be calculated using the methodology specified in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.2.2 - F1 Function.

core_children.core_name: Calculate Required Capital Percentage Using Piecewise Function With F1 Thresholds
core_children.core_content: The Required Capital Percentage should then be calculated by inputting the F1 parameter calculated in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.1.1.1 - Calculate F1 Parameter into the Piecewise Function specified in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.1 - Piecewise Function using the F1 thresholds specified in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.1.5.3 - Kink Threshold.

Medium risk protocols are ones with a Risk Rating greater than [code]25[/code] but less than or equal to [code]50[/code]. The methodology for medium risk protocols is specified in the documents herein.
For medium risk protocols, the F1 parameter $f_{1}$ should first be calculated using the methodology specified in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.2.2 - F1 Function.
The F2 parameter $f_{2}$ should then be calculated using the methodology specified in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.2.3 - F2 Function.

core_children.core_name: Calculate Required Capital Percentage Using F1 Piecewise Function
core_children.core_content: The Required Capital Percentage using the F1 Piecewise Function should then be calculated by inputting the F1 parameter calculated in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.1.2.1 - Calculate F1 Parameter into the Piecewise Function specified in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.1 - Piecewise Function using the F1 thresholds specified in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.1.5.3 - Kink Threshold.

core_children.core_name: Calculate Required Capital Percentage Using F2 Piecewise Function
core_children.core_content: The Required Capital Percentage using the F2 Piecewise Function should then be calculated by inputting the F2 parameter calculated in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.1.2.2 - Calculate F2 Parameter into the Piecewise Function specified in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.1 - Piecewise Function using the F2 thresholds specified in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.1.5.2 - Maximum Threshold.

core_children.core_name: Calculate Blended Average Required Capital Percentage
core_children.core_content:
The final Required Capital Percentage $x$ should be calculated as a weighted average of the F1 Required Capital Percentage and the F2 Required Capital Percentage as follows:

$$
x = b \times \alpha \times f_2 + (1 - \alpha) \times f_1
$$

The parameters of this formula are defined in the subdocuments herein.
The constant factor $b$ is set to [code]0.15[/code].

Alpha $\alpha$ is a weighting factor indicating how close the Risk Rating is to the threshold for a High Risk Protocol versus the threshold for a Low Risk Protocol. It is calculated as:

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{r - 25}{50 - 25}
$$

Here $r$ is the Risk Rating of the protocol.
The F1 Required Capital Percentage $f_1$ is the figure calculated as the output of A.3.3.2.1.2.3.1.2.3 - Calculate Required Capital Percentage Using F1 Piecewise Function.
The F2 Required Capital Percentage $f_2$ is the figure calculated as the output of A.3.3.2.1.2.3.1.2.4 - Calculate Required Capital Percentage Using F2 Piecewise Function.
High risk protocols are ones with a Risk Rating greater than [code]50[/code] but less than or equal to [code]75[/code]. The methodology for high risk protocols is specified in the documents herein.
For high risk protocols, the F2 parameter $f_{2}$ should first be calculated using the methodology specified in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.2.3 - F2 Function.

core_children.core_name: Calculate Required Capital Percentage Using Piecewise Function With F2 Thresholds
core_children.core_content: The Required Capital Percentage should then be calculated by inputting the F2 parameter calculated in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.1.3.1 - Calculate F2 Parameter into the Piecewise Function specified in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.1 - Piecewise Function using the F2 thresholds specified in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.1.5.2 - Maximum Threshold.
Extreme risk protocols are ones with a Risk Rating greater than [code]75[/code]. The methodology for extreme risk protocols is specified in the documents herein.
For extreme risk protocols, the required capital percentage is automatically set to 100%.

core_children.core_name: Calculation Of Instance Smart Contract RRC Value
core_children.core_content: The Instance Smart Contract RRC is equal to the product of (1) the Instance Smart Contract RRC Percentage calculated from A.3.3.2.1.2.3.1 - Calculation Of Instance Smart Contract RRC Percentage Based on Risk Category and (2) the exposure to the Allocation System opportunity.

core_children.core_name: Inputs To Instance Smart Contract RRC Calculations
core_children.core_content: The documents herein define functions and parameters that are used in multiple parts of the methodology for calculating the Instance Smart Contract RRC based on the Smart Contract Risk Rating specified in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.1 - Calculation Of Instance Smart Contract RRC Percentage Based on Risk Category.

The Piecewise Function $RCC(x)$ calculates a percentage risk capital requirement based on an input $x$ and is defined as follows:

$$
\text{RCC}(x) =\begin{cases}a & \text{if } x \le x_{\text{start}}, \\[8pt]b \times \dfrac{x - x_{\text{start}}}{x_{\text{kink}} - x_{\text{start}}} & \text{if } x_{\text{start}} < x \le x_{\text{kink}}, \\[8pt]b \;+\; c \;\dfrac{x - x_{\text{kink}}}{x_{\text{max}} - x_{\text{kink}}}& \text{if } x_{\text{kink}} < x < x_{\text{max}}, \\[8pt]d& \text{if } x \ge x_{\text{max}}.\end{cases}
$$
The low risk parameter $a$ is the output of the piecewise function when the input is at or below the starting threshold. The $a$ parameter is set to [code]0[/code].
The medium risk parameter $b$ is the output of the piecewise function when the input is equal to the kink threshold. The $b$ parameter is set to [code]0.25[/code].

The high risk parameter $c$ is the incremental value above the $b$ parameter that the piecewise function will output when the input is equal to the maximum threshold. The $c$ parameter is set to [code]0.75[/code].
The extreme risk parameter $d$ is the output of the piecewise function when the input is equal to or greater than the maximum threshold. The $d$ parameter is set to [code]1[/code].
The documents herein define the thresholds for the piecewise function.

The Starting Threshold $x_{\text{start}}$ is calculated as follows:

$$
x_{\text{start}}(r) = i \times (r_i - r)
$$

Here $r$ is the Risk Rating.

The parameters of this formula are defined in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.1.5.4 - Threshold Parameters.

The Maximum Threshold $x_{\text{max}}$ is calculated as follows:

$$
x_{\text{max}}(r) = x_{\text{start}}(r) + i_{max}
$$

Here $r$ is the Risk Rating.

The parameters of this formula are defined in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.1.5.4 - Threshold Parameters.

The Kink Threshold $x_{\text{kink}}$ is calculated as follows:

$$
x_{\text{kink}}(r) = x_{\text{start}}(r) \;+\; i_{kink} \times\bigl[\;x_{\text{max}}(r)\;-\;x_{\text{start}}(r)\bigr]
$$

Here $r$ is the Risk Rating.

The parameters of this formula are defined in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.1.5.4 - Threshold Parameters.

The documents herein define inputs that are used to calculate the Starting Threshold, Maximum Threshold, and Kink Threshold. These parameters differ depending on whether the piecewise function is invoked with F1 threshold parameters or F2 threshold parameters.
The documents herein define the F1 threshold parameters.
The value of the Rating Point Scaling Coefficient $i$ is [code]0.01[/code].
The value of the Reference Inflection Risk Rating $r_i$ is [code]50[/code].
The value of the Maximum Interval Width $i_{max}$ is [code]0.50[/code].
The value of the Kink Location Fraction $i_{kink}$ is [code]0.75[/code].
The documents herein define the F2 threshold parameters.
The value of the Rating Point Scaling Coefficient $i$ is [code]0.02[/code].
The value of the Reference Inflection Risk Rating $r_i$ is [code]75[/code].
The value of the Maximum Interval Width $i_{max}$ is [code]0.50[/code].
The value of the Kink Location Fraction $i_{kink}$ is [code]0.75[/code].
The documents herein define the F1 and F2 functions.
The documents herein define variables used in the F1 and F2 functions.
The Internal Exposure $e_{\text{int}}$ is the Agent’s allocation to the protocol.
The Total Exposure $e_{\text{tot}}$ is the Sky Ecosystem’s aggregate allocation to the protocol.
The Internal Liquid Surplus $s_{\text{liq}}$ is equal to the Total Risk Capital of the Agent.
The Exposure Beyond Surplus $E_{\text{beyond}}$ is the difference between the Sky Ecosystem’s aggregate allocation to the protocol and aggregate internal liquid surplus of all Agents.
The Total Collateral $C_{\text{tot}}$ is the total USDS and Dai debt in the system.

The F1 parameter $f_{1}$ is calculated as follows:

$$
f_{1} = \dfrac{E_{\text{beyond}}}{C_{\text{tot}}}
$$

The parameters of this function are defined in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.2.1 - Variable Definitions.

The F2 parameter $f_2$ is calculated as follows:

$$
f_{2} = \dfrac{e_{\text{int}} + a \,\bigl(e_{\text{tot}} - e_{\text{int}}\bigr)}{s_{\text{liq}}}
$$

Here $\alpha$ is equal to [code]0.1[/code].

The parameters of this function are defined in A.3.3.2.1.2.3.3.2.1 - Variable Definitions.

The document herein contains a reference implementation of the calculation of Instance Smart Contract RRC based on the Smart Contract Risk Rating.

[code]import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# ------------------------------------------------------------------
# Function Definition
# ------------------------------------------------------------------

def piecewise_coverage(x, x_start, x_kink, x_max):
    """
    Returns a coverage fraction in [0.0 ... 1.0] given x
    and piecewise linear thresholds:
      - 0% coverage if x < x_start
      - linear 0%→25% from x_start to x_kink
      - linear 25%→100% from x_kink to x_max
      - 100% if x > x_max
    """
    # Safely handle edge cases
    if x = x_max:
        return 1.0
    
    # If between x_start and x_kink -> map linearly from 0% to 25%
    if x_start < x  0.25 (25%)
        return 0.25 * frac
    
    # If between x_kink and x_max -> map linearly from 25% to 100%
    else:  # x_kink < x < x_max
        span = x_max - x_kink
        frac = (x - x_kink) / span
        # coverage goes 0.25 -> 1.0
        return 0.25 + (0.75 * frac)
        
# ------------------------------------------------------------------
# Thresholds
# ------------------------------------------------------------------

def f1_thresholds_low_risk(rating):
    """
    Return (x_start, x_kink, x_max)
    in % terms, e.g. 5%, 23.75%, 30%, etc.
    """
    x_start = (50 - rating) * 0.01  # e.g. rating=0 -> 0.5 (50%)
    x_max   = x_start + 0.50       # always a 50% gap to max
    # kink is 75% of the way from start to max
    x_kink  = x_start + 0.75 * (x_max - x_start)
    return (x_start, x_kink, x_max)

def f2_thresholds_high_risk(rating):
    """
    Return (x_start, x_kink, x_max)
    in fraction form (0.50, 0.875, 1.00, etc).
    """
    # starting threshold = (75 - rating) * 2%:
    x_start = (75 - rating) * 0.02
    x_max = x_start + 0.50
    x_kink = x_start + 0.75 * (x_max - x_start)  # 75% of the way
    return (x_start, x_kink, x_max)

# ------------------------------------------------------------------
# RRC Calculation
# ------------------------------------------------------------------

def calculate_rrc_coverage(
    rating,
    exposure_internal,
    exposure_total,
    liquid_surplus_internal,
    total_exposure_beyond_surplus,
    total_collateral
):
    """
    Returns the coverage fraction (0 - 1) for a given rating & scenario.
    For the absolute coverage amount, multiply coverage_fraction
    by `exposure_internal`.
    """

    # 1) Compute the two driver ratios
    f1 = max(0.0, total_exposure_beyond_surplus / total_collateral) # Ensuring it is non-negative when surplus > exposure
    f2 = (exposure_internal + 0.1 * (exposure_total - exposure_internal)) / liquid_surplus_internal

    # 2) Determine which category the rating is in
    if rating  use only f1 piecewise
        x_start, x_kink, x_max = f1_thresholds_low_risk(rating)
        coverage_fraction = piecewise_coverage(f1, x_start, x_kink, x_max)

    elif rating  blend coverage from f1-curve and f2-curve
        # First get coverage from the "f1 low-risk"
        x_start_1, x_kink_1, x_max_1 = f1_thresholds_low_risk(25) 
        cov_f1 = piecewise_coverage(f1, x_start_1, x_kink_1, x_max_1)

        # Then get coverage from the "f2 high-risk" 
        x_start_2, x_kink_2, x_max_2 = f2_thresholds_high_risk(50)
        cov_f2 = piecewise_coverage(f2, x_start_2, x_kink_2, x_max_2)

        # Blend factor alpha
        alpha = (rating - 25) / (50 - 25)
        coverage_fraction = (1 - alpha) * cov_f1 + 0.15 * alpha * cov_f2 

    elif rating  use only f2
        x_start, x_kink, x_max = f2_thresholds_high_risk(rating)
        coverage_fraction = piecewise_coverage(f2, x_start, x_kink, x_max)

    else:
        # TRR Category 4 (Extreme Risk) => special piecewise lumps.  
        if rating > 75:
            return 1.0

    return coverage_fraction
[/code]
The documents herein define exceptions to the calculation of Instance Smart Contract RRC for specific protocols.
The Smart Contract Risk Rating for Maple is [code]20[/code].
The Smart Contract Risk Rating for Fluid is [code]25[/code].
The documents herein define the implementation of the risk framework for calculating Instance Administrative RRC.

The first step in calculating the Instance Administrative RRC with respect to an Allocation System opportunity is to calculate the Administrative Risk Rating $\text{ARR}$ for the protocol being invested in. The $\text{ARR}$ is calculated as follows:

$$
\text{ARR} = min[\text{CAP}, \text{SR} \times \text{DF} \times \text{LAF}]
$$

Here $min$ is the mathematical minimum function that returns the lesser of the specified parameters.

The parameters of this formula are specified in the subdocuments herein.
The Administrative Risk Rating Cap $\text{CAP}$ is a temporary cap on the Administrative Risk Rating. The value of the $\text{CAP}$ is [code]30[/code].

The Starting Rate $\text{SR}$ is an initial risk rating for Administrative Risk before taking into account the Delay Factor and Lindy Adjustment Factor. It is a function of the type of backdoor access that exists to the protocol, as specified in the documents herein.

A protocol with no backdoor access allows no privileged access to the relevant smart contracts by a whitelisted set of users. The Starting Rate for a protocol with no backdoor access is [code]0[/code].

A protocol with limited backdoor access allows a set of privileged users to materially modify the terms of the smart contracts (e.g. freezing the transfer of funds) but does not allow root backdoor access as specified in A.3.3.2.1.3.1.2.3 - Root Backdoor. The Starting Rate for a protocol with limited backdoor access is [code]50[/code].

A protocol with root backdoor access allows a set of privileged users to make arbitrary changes to the terms of the smart contracts, including transferring user funds. The Starting Rate for a protocol with root backdoor access is [code]100[/code].

The Delay Adjustment Factor $\text{DF}$ is a factor indicating the extent to which the risk associated with backdoor access is mitigated by a security delay between the time that a change using backdoor access is approved and the time that such a change becomes effective. This delay gives users time to raise issues or withdraw funds in the event of malicious or undesirable use of backdoor access.

The Delay Factor is [code]1[/code] if there is no security delay and [code]0[/code] if the security delay is 48 hours or greater. For security delays between 0 hours and 48 hours, the Delay Factor is linearly reduced for each hour of security delay. So a security delay of 24 hours would result in a Delay Factor of [code]0.5[/code].
The Lindy Adjustment Factor $\text{LAF}$ is calculated using the same methodology specified in A.3.3.2.1.2.2.4 - Lindy Adjustment Factor.

The second step in calculating the Instance Administrative RRC with respect to an Allocation System opportunity is to calculate the Instance Administrative RRC as a function of the Administrative Risk Rating. This is done using the same methodology as specified in A.3.3.2.1.2.3 - Smart Contract Risk Required Risk Capital Calculation.
The documents herein define exceptions to the calculation of Instance Administrative RRC for specific protocols.
The Administrative Risk Rating for Maple is [code]20[/code].
The Administrative Risk Rating for Fluid is [code]25[/code].

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define the core risk capital parameters.

core_children.core_content: This document specifies the JRC loss allocation parameters.

The Tip JRC amount is 10% of the Star Agent's Total Junior Risk Capital (IJRC + SEJRC + TEJRC).

For example, if a Star Agent has $20 million of Internal Junior Risk Capital and $20 million of Star External Junior Risk Capital, the Tip Junior Risk Capital would be $4 million.
This document specifies the values of the Risk Capital sourcing ratios defined in A.3.3.1.2.3 - Total Risk Capital Sourcing Ratios.

The External Per Internal (EPI) ratio is 1.00. For example, if a Star Agent has $20 million of Internal Junior Risk Capital, the EPI ratio enables it to source $20 million of External Junior Risk Capital.

This EPI ratio governs the initial mechanism for sourcing EJRC (SEJRC + TEJRC) and is based purely on the Star's IJRC; Stars can source EJRC via this initial mechanism up to a maximum amount equal to their IJRC holdings. However, additional EJRC may potentially be acquired via the alternative use of SPJ capacity, as described in A.3.3.1.2.3.1.2.1 -  Alternative Use Of SPJ Capacity To Source External Junior Risk Capital.

The SPJ ratio varies by the JRC type used for enablement:

• Internal Junior Risk Capital SPJ: 1.00,
• EPI-acquired Star External Junior Risk Capital SPJ: 0.75,
• EPI-acquired Tokenized External Junior Risk Capital SPJ: 0.50,
• SPJ-acquired External Junior Risk Capital (SEJRC or TEJRC): zero (0).

For example, if a Star Agent has $20 million of Internal Junior Risk Capital, $10 million of Star External Junior Risk Capital, and $10 million of Tokenized External Junior Risk Capital, then it would be able to count or “enable” $32.5 million of Senior Capital towards its required Risk Capital.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define operational requirements and protocols for the implementation of the Junior Risk Capital system in the risk framework.

core_children.core_name: Star External JRC Rental System Implementation
core_children.core_content: The implementation of the SEJRC rental system is defined as a Sky Primitive, specifically, the Junior Risk Capital Rental Primitive. See A.2.3.9.2 - Junior Risk  Capital Rental Primitive.
This document will be further developed in a future iteration of the Atlas.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define operational requirements and protocols for the implementation of the Senior Risk Capital system in the risk framework.

core_children.core_name: Sourcing Of Internal Senior Risk Capital
core_children.core_content: ISRC is sourced from the various surplus buffers, which are themselves funded via the Treasury Management Function. See A.2.4.1.2 - Allocation Steps.

core_children.core_name: External Senior Risk Capital And srUSDS System
core_children.core_content: The documents herein define rules and infrastructure pertaining to the sourcing of External Senior Risk Capital (ESRC). ESRC is capital provided by external investors to be invested as Senior Risk Capital of Star Agents.

srUSDS is the tokenized form of External Senior Risk Capital. Users can deposit USDS into the srUSDS contract to provide External Senior Risk Capital to the SRC system, making it available for origination by Star Agents. See A.3.3.2.4.3 - Senior Risk Capital (SRC) Origination Process. srUSDS holders can potentially earn higher yields in exchange for the risk that the External Senior Risk Capital will suffer losses.

The srUSDS contract utilizes distinct queues for managing conversions. Users can add USDS to the deposit queue for conversion into srUSDS; or add srUSDS to the redemption queue for conversion back into USDS at any point during the month.

Assets placed in either queue can be withdrawn by the user at any time before the Monthly Settlement Cycle begins.

At the Monthly Settlement Cycle, all assets remaining in the queues are processed: queued USDS is converted into srUSDS, and queued srUSDS is converted into USDS based on the prevailing Conversion Rate. See A.3.3.2.4.2.3 - Conversion Rate.

The conversion rate represents the amount of USDS redeemable per unit of srUSDS. This rate increases or decreases over time, reflecting the net return earned by the ESRC pool. The rate is updated at the conclusion of each Monthly Settlement Cycle based on the performance of the ESRC pool during the preceding month, according to the following standard compounding formula:

[code]New srUSDS Conversion Rate = Previous Conversion Rate * (1 + Monthly srUSDS Yield)[/code]

The parameters of this formula are defined in the documents herein.

[code]Previous Conversion Rate[/code] is the conversion rate (USDS per srUSDS) that was in effect at the beginning of the concluded monthly cycle. The initial conversion rate between srUSDS and USDS is 1:1.

[code]Monthly srUSDS Yield[/code] represents the net percentage return earned solely by the ESRC pool during the concluded monthly cycle, distributable to srUSDS holders via the conversion rate adjustment. It directly reflects the risk-adjusted performance of the capital deployed and is calculated as:

[code][(Total Interest Paid by Stars on ESRC Portion - Losses Allocated to ESRC) / ESRC Principal at start of month - Sky Spread] * (1 - ESRC Earnings Fee)[/code]

The Sky Spread and ESRC Earnings Fee are defined in A.3.3.2.4.2.3.3.1 - Sky Spread and A.3.3.2.4.2.3.3.2 - ESRC Earnings Fee, respectively.
Sky earns revenue from facilitating ESRC from two sources. These sources are included in the formula and defined in the subdocuments below.

Sky takes a spread equal to the Sky Spread on all ESRC balances. The Sky Spread is the difference between (1) the rate at which Star Agents borrow from Sky and (2) the Sky Savings Rate plus the Accessibility Reward Fee.
Sky takes a 5% fee on the net interest earnings generated from the ESRC portion of originated SRC before these earnings are distributed to srUSDS holders via the conversion rate adjustment.

Star Agents and third-party partners that drive srUSDS usage are entitled to a srUSDS Accessibility Reward similar to the Accessibility Reward paid on USDS balances. The srUSDS Accessibility Reward Fee is the same as the Accessibility Reward Fee on USDS. See A.2.3.8.1 - Accessibility Reward Primitive.

This document defines the operational details of the recurring monthly process allowing Star Agents to originate Senior Risk Capital (Originated Senior Risk Capital or OSRC) from the Total Senior Risk Capital (TSRC) pool. The process involves determining the available TSRC supply, a bidding phase, an allocation auction, and settlement aligned with the Monthly Settlement Cycle.

The SRC origination process operates monthly, synchronized with the Monthly Settlement Cycle. The bidding window occurs during the current month for SRC that is usable in the next month; the bidding window closes before settlement processing begins. Additional operational details, including timelines, will be specified in a future iteration of the Atlas.

core_children.core_name: Available Total Senior Risk Capital Pool Determination
core_children.core_content: Total Senior Risk Capital (TSRC) is the amount of Senior Risk Capital that is available each Monthly Settlement Cycle for Stars to originate and thus turn into Originated Senior Risk Capital (OSRC) through the monthly origination process. TSRC is the sum of designated Internal Senior Risk Capital (ISRC) from Sky surplus buffers and available External Senior Risk Capital (ESRC) from the srUSDS contract. Prior to each monthly bidding window, the TSRC is calculated and publicly announced. Additional operational details will be defined in a future iteration of the Atlas.

Participating Star Agents submit bids via the Powerhouse interface during the defined bidding window. Each bid must specify: (1) The quantity of SRC the Star seeks to originate, and (2) The maximum price the Star is willing to pay (expressed in basis points above the Base Rate) for using the OSRC during the upcoming monthly period. Additional operational details will be specified in a future iteration of the Atlas.

Bids are ranked descending by price. TSRC is allocated sequentially to the highest bids until the pool is exhausted or all bids are filled. The price of the lowest successful bid that receives an allocation (even partial) sets the uniform clearing price for the month. All winning bidders pay this clearing price per unit of OSRC allocated, regardless of their original bid price. Additional operational details will be specified in a future iteration of the Atlas.

During the Monthly Settlement Cycle following allocation, the cost (Allocated OSRC Quantity * Clearing Price) is deducted from each winning Star Agent’s operational account. Concurrently, the allocated OSRC amount is credited to the Star Agent’s Risk Capital account as OSRC for the upcoming monthly period.

OSRC is valid only for the single monthly period following the settlement cycle in which it was originated. Renewal requires successful participation in the next monthly origination process; there is no automatic rollover.

core_children.core_name: Originated Senior Risk Capital (OSRC) Rental Implementation
core_children.core_content: The operational process for inter-Star Agent OSRC risk-capital rentals is defined herein. SEJRC rentals are facilitated through the Junior Risk Capital Rental Primitive. See A.2.3.9.2 - Junior Risk  Capital Rental Primitive.

Rental of Originated Senior Risk Capital (OSRC) requires a registered Ecosystem Accord specifying: Amount, Duration, and Price/Terms. As the system permits sub-renting (allowing a Star to rent out OSRC it has previously rented), the Accord must explicitly state if the borrower is granted this permission. In regards to Duration, the Ecosystem Accord must specify the rental period, which cannot extend beyond the end of the current Monthly Settlement Cycle. Stars may agree on any Duration within this limit.

Standardized Ecosystem Accord templates will be provided in a future iteration of the Atlas.

A registered OSRC-rental Ecosystem Accord effectively transfers the claim on the specified amount of the lender’s OSRC to the borrower for the duration of the agreement. The borrowing Star Agent must possess sufficient Junior Risk Capital (IJRC, SEJRC, TEJRC) and corresponding Senior Per Junior (SPJ) capacity to enable the rented OSRC, such that it counts towards their own Risk Capital requirements. Rented OSRC that is not successfully enabled by the borrower pursuant to the SPJ ratio does not contribute to their Risk Capital calculation.

As an interim measure, prior to the implementation of the Senior Risk Capital System, Sky Core may provide Senior Risk Capital to Star Agents under Ecosystem Accords between Sky Core and the respective Star Agents. The terms of such Ecosystem Accords shall be documented under A.2.9 - Ecosystem Accords.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define the process for updating Risk Capital requirements.

core_children.core_content: The documents herein define the requirements for updating inputs to the calculation of Risk Capital requirements.

Inputs into Risk Capital calculations that are based on market or other external variables should be updated in real time or immediately after the underlying data is updated, if the underlying data is only updated periodically.

core_children.core_name: Responsibility For Cost Of Real Time Updates
core_children.core_content: Star Agents may develop data feeds that update the Atlas with their data at their expense. Alternatively, the Operational Executor Agent that has an Executor Accord with the Star Agent may perform this as part of the services it provides to the Star Agent. In this case, the Operational Executor Agent may charge whatever fees for this service the parties mutually agree on.

core_children.core_name: Near Term Real Time Updates By BA Labs
core_children.core_content: In the near term, BA Labs will provide real time data regarding major lending markets. BA Labs will be compensated for this work as part of its existing relationship with Sky Core and Spark.

In the near term, Star Agents may update calculations that are based on market or other external variables less frequently than real time. If they do so, Operational GovOps must apply a buffer to each variable that is not updated in real time to account for the fact that the variable may change in a way that increases Required Risk Capital between update frequencies.
The documents herein define processes to maintain and ensure the integrity of data that is used as an input to Risk Capital calculations.

The Star Agent may assume responsibility for updating the inputs to the Risk Capital calculations itself, either directly or through one of its agents, or may contract with Operational GovOps to perform that function.
If Operational GovOps does not perform the data updates, it must verify their accuracy.

In the near term, Operational GovOps must reduce exposure to any Asset Allocation Conduit if the Star Agent responsible for that Conduit fails to provide the timely information necessary to calculate the required Risk Capital. In the future, a system of monetary penalties must be developed to compensate Sky for the risk posed by late data submission.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define how changes to the calculation of Required Risk Capital or Total Risk Capital should be handled.

core_children.core_name: Updates Due To Changed Market Parameters
core_children.core_content: The documents herein define the process for handling changes to Required Risk Capital requirements due to changes in market or other external variables.

core_children.core_name: Immediate Update To Required Risk Capital For Changes In Market Or External Variables
core_children.core_content: To the extent that a market or external variable changes in a way that increases the Aggregate Required Risk Capital, the Star Agent is immediately responsible for supplying additional Total Risk Capital, as necessary, and is subject to penalties for any period in which this Total Risk Capital is not provided. It is the responsibility of the Star Agent to maintain sufficient capital so that they remain well capitalized in the face of changes to market conditions.

core_children.core_name: Updates Due To Changed Methodology Or Governance Parameters
core_children.core_content: The documents herein define the process for adjusting the calculation of Required Risk Capital or Total Risk Capital whenever Sky Governance modifies the underlying methodology/parameters governing Risk Capital calculations.

core_children.core_name: Phased Update To Risk Capital For Changes In Methodology Or Governance Parameters
core_children.core_content: To the extent that a change to the implementation of the Risk Capital framework or a parameter set by Sky Governance changes the Required Risk Capital or Total Risk Capital, the change will phase in over time. The Star Agent must provide 50% of the additional Total Risk Capital necessary to comply with the change within seven (7) calendar days of the change, and the remaining 50% on a pro rata basis over the following twenty one (21) calendar days.

core_children.core_name: No Penalties For Changes In Methodology Or Governance Parameters In Near Term
core_children.core_content: In the near term as the Risk Capital implementation continues to be developed, Star Agents will not be subject to penalties for failing to supply the incremental capital required by changes to the Risk Capital implementation. However, Operational GovOps must reduce the exposure to Allocation Conduits managed by the Star Agent to bring it back into compliance with capital requirements if the Star Agent does not work in good faith to provide the additional capital on the timeline specified above.

section_primary_docs.core_content: The documents herein define monitoring and penalty mechanisms.

core_children.core_name: Monitoring As Part Of Settlement Cycle
core_children.core_content: Core GovOps reviews the calculation of Risk Capital by each Star Agent as part of the Settlement Cycle. In the event that it detects that Risk Capital requirements were violated, it applies the penalty mechanisms specified in A.3.3.2.7.2 - Penalty Mechanisms.

The documents herein specify the penalty mechanisms that exist to incentivize Star Agents and Operational Agents to ensure that sufficient capital is always held against investments by Star Agents and to protect Sky from risks due to insufficient capital.
The documents herein specify the penalty mechanisms for Star Agents for breaches of the Risk Capital requirements.

core_children.core_name: Financial Penalties For Breach Of Capital Requirements
core_children.core_content: The documents herein define the financial penalties for breaches of Risk Capital requirements.
The Risk Tolerance Ratio for a Star Agent is the ratio of its Aggregate Required Risk Capital to its Total Risk Capital.

The financial penalties for breaches of Risk Capital requirements depend on whether the breaches are Low Severity or High Severity. The documents herein define Low Severity and High Severity Breaches. The financial penalties associated with High Severity Breaches and Low Severity Breaches are defined in A.3.3.2.7.2.1.1.4 - Financial Penalties For Low Severity Breaches and A.3.3.2.7.2.1.1.5 - Financial Penalties For High Severity Breaches, respectively.
A Low Severity Breach is one in which the Risk Tolerance Ratio is greater than or equal to 97% but less than 100%.
A High Severity Breach is one in which the Risk Tolerance Ratio is less than 97%.

The length of a breach refers to the duration measured between its defined start and end points, which depend on the specific aspect of the breach being considered.

If a breach is initially a Low Severity Breach and subsequently becomes a High Severity Breach, then (1) the length of the Low Severity Breach is the duration from when it became a Low Severity Breach until it became a High Severity Breach; and (2) the length of the High Severity Breach is the duration from when it became a High Severity Breach until that high-severity period concludes (e.g., upon final resolution of the breach or a transition to a different severity level).

Likewise, if a breach is initially a High Severity Breach and subsequently becomes a Low Severity Breach, then (1) the length of the High Severity Breach is the duration from when it became a High Severity Breach until it became a Low Severity Breach; and (2) the length of the Low Severity Breach is the duration from when it became a Low Severity Breach until that low-severity period concludes (e.g., upon final resolution of the breach or a transition to a different severity level).

core_children.core_name: Financial Penalties For Low Severity Breaches
core_children.core_content: The documents herein define the financial penalties for Low Severity Breaches.
For the first 30 minutes of a Low Severity Breach, the financial penalty is equal to a 500% annual percentage yield on the difference between Aggregate Required Risk Capital and Total Risk Capital.

For the next 30 minutes of a Low Severity Breach after the expiration of the duration specified in A.3.3.2.7.2.1.1.4.1 - Financial Penalties For First 30 Minutes, the financial penalty is equal to a 1,000% annual percentage yield on the difference between Aggregate Required Risk Capital and Total Risk Capital.

After the first 60 minutes of a Low Severity Breach, the financial penalty is equal to a 1,500% annual percentage yield on the difference between Aggregate Required Risk Capital and Total Risk Capital.

core_children.core_name: Financial Penalties For High Severity Breaches
core_children.core_content: The documents herein define the financial penalties for High Severity Breaches.

For the first 15 minutes of a High Severity Breach, the financial penalty is equal to a 1,500% annual percentage yield on the difference between Aggregate Required Risk Capital and Total Risk Capital.

For the next 15 minutes of a High Severity Breach after the expiration of the duration specified in A.3.3.2.7.2.1.1.5.1 - Financial Penalties For First 15 Minutes, the financial penalty is equal to a 2,000% annual percentage yield on the difference between Aggregate Required Risk Capital and Total Risk Capital.

For the next 30 minutes of a High Severity Breach after the expiration of the duration specified in A.3.3.2.7.2.1.1.5.2 - Financial Penalties For Subsequent 15 Minutes, the financial penalty is equal to a 2,500% annual percentage yield on the difference between Aggregate Required Risk Capital and Total Risk Capital.

After the first 60 minutes of a High Severity Breach, the financial penalty is equal to a 3,000% annual percentage yield on the difference between Aggregate Required Risk Capital and Total Risk Capital.

Penalties are calculated by the Core Executor Agent as part of the Monthly Settlement Cycle. See A.2.5 - Sky Core Monthly Settlement Cycle. Once calculated, penalties are transferred from the Star Agent’s SubProxy Account to Sky by the Star Agent’s Operational Executor Agent.

In the event that the Star Agent’s Total Risk Capital is less than its Required Risk Capital at the time of the Monthly Settlement Cycle, or the collection of the penalty would cause the Star Agent’s Total Risk Capital to fall below its Required Risk Capital, then Sky may exercise the alternative enforcement mechanism described below.

In this instance Sky, at its discretion, may issue additional tokens of the Star Agent and sell them on the open market until it has collected proceeds equal to the calculated penalty. Any proceeds above the calculated penalty shall be refunded to the Star Agent SubProxy account.

Alternatively, Sky may convert the penalty into a debt owed by the Star Agent to Sky Core at terms mutually agreed between Sky Core and the Star Agent.

While the risk framework undergoes refinement, penalties for any Star Agent breaches of Required Risk Capital will be calculated by Core GovOps once the infrastructure is in place to do so. This calculation serves the purposes of system monitoring, data gathering, and framework calibration. However, throughout this interim phase, such calculated penalties will not be formally assessed against Star Agents, and consequently, Star Agents will not be required to make payment for these penalties. Notwithstanding this temporary exemption from penalty payment, Star Agents are expected to maintain a Risk Tolerance Ratio of at least 90%. See A.3.3.2.7.2.1.1.1 - Risk Tolerance Ratio.

core_children.core_name: Additional Token Issuance For Breach Of Capital Requirements
core_children.core_content: In the event that financial penalties are not sufficient to address the shortfall because payment of the penalties would exacerbate an existing capital shortfall, Core GovOps may require the Star Agent to issue additional tokens and sell them to the extent it deems necessary. Operational GovOps will assist Core GovOps in executing any such transaction. Future iterations of the Risk Capital framework will specify a more detailed methodology for required token issuance.

core_children.core_name: Restrictions On Investments For Breach Of Capital Requirement
core_children.core_content: Core GovOps may impose whatever restrictions it deems necessary on the usage of the Allocation System Primitive by the Star Agent, including limiting the Star Agent’s exposure to certain Instances (conduits) or prohibiting the Star Agent from using certain Instances. These restrictions may extend for a period of time that Core GovOps deems fit.

core_children.core_name: Conservatorship For Breach Of Capital Requirements
core_children.core_content: In the event that less extreme measures are not adequate to address the capital shortfall, Core GovOps may seek to put the Star Agent into conservatorship, in which case Sky Core Facilitators take direct control of the Star Agent to maximize value for Sky and other Star Agent stakeholders. Seeking to put a Star Agent into conservatorship requires immediate escalation to Sky Core Governance and requires an expedited Executive Vote as specified in A.1.8.1.5.4.1 - Known And Uncontentious Remedies.

core_children.core_name: Penalty Mechanisms For Operational Executor Agents
core_children.core_content: The documents herein specify the penalty mechanisms for Operational Executor Agents for breaches of Risk Capital requirements.

core_children.core_name: Financial Penalties For Operational Executor Agents
core_children.core_content: In the near term there are no penalties for Operational Executor Agents for failure of the Star Agents they supervise to maintain appropriate levels of capital. In future iterations of the Risk Framework, Operational Executor Agents will be required to put up collateral and act as “insurers” of all activities by the Star Agents they supervise with regard to the Sky Primitives. At that point Core GovOps will be able to seize this collateral in the event of failure to obtain compensation for breaches of the Risk Capital requirement by the Star Agent.

In the event that Core GovOps determines that the Operational Executor Agent is not appropriately supervising the activities of the Star Agent, Core GovOps may terminate the respective Executor Accord. This requires an immediate escalation to Sky Governance and requires an expedited Executive Vote as specified in A.1.8.1.5.4.1 - Known And Uncontentious Remedies. Core GovOps must arrange for another Operational Executor Agent to stand in until the Star Agent can Invoke the Executor Accord Primitive to enter into a new Executor Accord. The Star Agent must suspend all operations until a new Executor Accord is voted on and formally integrated into the Agent’s Artifact.
The documents herein specify measures for escalating situations directly to Sky Governance in the event that they cannot be resolved by Core GovOps.

In the event of a disagreement regarding the penalties for a violation of Risk Capital requirements, either party may escalate the matter to Sky Governance. In addition, the extraordinary remedies of Star Agent Conservatorship and Executor Accord Termination must always be escalated to a Sky Governance Executive Vote.

Once a matter has been escalated to Sky Governance, the Sky Core Facilitators may request any information they deem necessary from Core GovOps, Operational GovOps, and the Star Agent. Sky Governance then acts through a Governance Poll to determine its resolution of the matter.

scope_articles.article_content: This Article defines rules pertaining to Sky’s Collateral Portfolio, which consists of several different assets to ensure liquidity.

article_sections.section_content: This Section governs the management of Sky’s Collateral Portfolio.

section_primary_docs.core_content: Sky must maintain a reserve of at least 20% of the total Dai and USDS Supply held as Cash Stablecoins, or assets and exposure that can rapidly be converted to Cash Stablecoins.

core_children.core_content:
A Cash Stablecoin is defined as either a Stablecoin listed in A.3.4.1.1.1.1 - List Of Cash Stablecoin; a technically secure DeFi token that can be instantly converted to a listed Stablecoin; or a custody solution that can be converted into a listed Stablecoin within 30 minutes.
